title: From Report to Labs
text:
It follows the main report rather than replacing it.
It turns the report’s argument into a reusable lab checklist.
It gives the group a shared standard before the datasets change.


Frame this as a bridge session. The audience has already seen the main report, so the job here is not to repeat every method detail. The job is to stabilize the design logic they should carry into the labs.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
It follows the main report rather than replacing it.
It turns the report’s argument into a reusable lab checklist.
It gives the group a shared standard before the datasets change.


Frame this as a bridge session. The audience has already seen the main report, so the job here is not to repeat every method detail. The job is to stabilize the design logic they should carry into the labs.

title: From Report to Labs
section: 2 What The Main Report Already Established
text:
2 What The Main Report Already Established

Start with the causal question and estimand, not the software.
Judge balance together with overlap, retention, and weight concentration.
Treat matching as one option inside a wider quasi-experimental toolkit.
Read every adjusted estimate as design-contingent rather than automatic.


This slide should sound familiar. Use it to remind the room that the labs are extensions of the same argument, not separate exercises with a different standard.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
3 What The Labs Are For

Re-run the same design-first workflow on new datasets.
See how method costs change when overlap is weaker or the data structure changes.
Practice defending design choices out loud, not just running code to completion.


Make the pedagogic point explicit: the labs are for judgement. A technically correct script that skips the design argument is still a weak answer.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
4 The Non-Negotiable Workflow

Define treatment, outcome, covariates, and estimand.
Inspect raw imbalance before adjusting anything.
Use exact matching as a transparency check where feasible.
Compare CEM and entropy balancing on the same target.
Estimate effects only after the design is defensible.


Deliver this as the lab contract. If participants jump straight to the outcome model or the final coefficient, bring them back to this sequence.

title: From Report to Labs
section: 5 Four Questions To Ask In Every Lab
text:
5 Four Questions To Ask In Every Lab

Who is the treated group we are trying to learn about?
What makes a control unit comparable before treatment?
How much design cost are we paying to improve comparability?
After adjustment, what population does the estimate actually describe?


These four questions are useful because they work across all three datasets. They also surface estimand drift early, before people get attached to a particular estimate.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
6 Reading Balance Without Fooling Yourself

Standardized mean differences near zero are useful, not sufficient.
Love plots help show whether imbalance is broad or concentrated.
Continuous covariates also need distribution checks, not just mean checks.
Good balance on the wrong covariates is still a weak design.


Slow down on the last point. Many learners assume a neat balance table proves design quality. Stress that the covariate set still has to be causally defensible and measured before treatment.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
7 Reading Design Cost








Design move
What improves
What you must still check




Matching
Visible comparability rules
Who got dropped and whether the treated target narrowed


Weighting
Retains the original rows
Effective sample size and whether a few units now dominate




This is the cleanest way to explain the matching-versus-weighting difference before the hands-on work starts. Matching makes sample restriction visible. Weighting keeps rows but can hide information loss inside the weights.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
8 Method Choice In Lab Terms

Exact matching: best as a feasibility check with a clear audit trail. Watch for continuous differences that still sit outside the match.
CEM: best matched-sample compromise when you want visible design rules. Watch for cutpoints that quietly redefine who remains comparable.
Entropy balancing: best when treated retention matters and mean balance must be strong. Watch for extreme weights and a collapsing control effective sample size.


Keep this practical rather than abstract. The point is not to rank methods universally. The point is to teach what each method is buying and where the hidden cost tends to appear.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
9 Common Failure Modes

Choosing a method before naming the estimand.
Pulling in variables that are post-treatment or ambiguous in timing.
Reporting the adjusted effect without reporting balance and overlap.
Treating nominal retention as proof that the target population stayed intact.
Ignoring weight dispersion because the balance table looks perfect.


Use this slide to inoculate the room against the most common shortcuts. If you want to make it concrete, mention that entropy balancing can keep every row while still reducing the effective control sample sharply.

title: From Report to Labs
section: 10 How The Lab Bundle Is Sequenced
text:
10 How The Lab Bundle Is Sequenced







Lab
What it is best for




Black Politicians
Structural imbalance, thin comparability, and method fragility


NSW + CPS Benchmark
Comparing observational designs against an experimental reference


Titanic
Compact teaching on support cells, exact matching, and subclassification




Explain the logic of the sequence. Black Politicians is the best immediate follow-on from the report because it keeps the design-first comparison visible. NSW plus CPS adds a benchmark question. Titanic is the short appendix when you want a simpler classroom example.

title: From Report to Labs
section: 11 What A Defensible Lab Answer Looks Like
text:
11 What A Defensible Lab Answer Looks Like

It states the estimand before any adjustment output.
It explains why the chosen covariates belong in the design.
It reports raw and adjusted diagnostics on the same scale.
It names the retention or weighting cost directly.
It says what causal claim is still out of reach.


This is the marking guide in slide form. End by reminding participants that a careful negative conclusion, such as “the data do not support this comparison cleanly enough,” is a good answer if the diagnostics point that way.

title: From Report to Labs
text:
12 Transition To The Labs

Start with the Black Politicians lab.
Move to the NSW + CPS benchmark lab when you want an external reference point.
Use the Titanic teaching lab for the shortest support-focused exercise.
Keep the main report open as the design reference.


Close with a simple handoff. Tell the audience which page to open first and what standard to keep in mind: define the target, inspect the raw comparison, then decide whether the design cost is acceptable.

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
By the end of the hour, participants should be able to say:
Matching and weighting are useful design tools, but they have to be judged by the tradeoff they impose between balance, overlap, retention, and weight concentration.

Use this as the anchor sentence for the whole session. If discussion wanders into software details or isolated estimates, return to this line.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
By the end of the hour, participants should be able to say:
Matching and weighting are useful design tools, but they have to be judged by the tradeoff they impose between balance, overlap, retention, and weight concentration.

Use this as the anchor sentence for the whole session. If discussion wanders into software details or isolated estimates, return to this line.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
2 What This Lab Is For

deliver the report’s main messages, not every technical detail
train design judgement rather than software mimicry
prepare the room for the follow-on labs


Open by lowering the pressure to cover everything. The point is to teach the report’s logic well enough that participants can carry it into later exercises.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
3 60-Minute Flow



Time
Focus




0-5
Opening frame


5-15
Core report argument


15-25
Design-first workflow


25-40
Method comparison


40-50
Interpreting the worked example


50-60
Discussion and handoff




Set expectations up front. This slide gives you permission to move the room along rather than turning the lab into a line-by-line report walkthrough.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
4 Opening Frame

This is a lab about design judgement, not button-pushing.
The methods solve related but not identical design problems.
The key question is not “which method wins?”
The key question is “what design cost are we willing to defend?”


Ask what usually gets reported first in matching write-ups: design or estimate. Use that to surface the report’s critique of outcome-first presentation.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
5 Three Messages To Land Early

Define the estimand before choosing the method.
Treat matching and weighting as design-stage tools.
Read balance together with retention and effective sample size.


If participants only remember three things, make it these. Repeat them at least twice during the hour.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
6 The Design-First Workflow

Identify the target as the ATT.
Define the treated group as programme participants.
Name the defensible pre-treatment covariates.
Inspect raw imbalance before any adjustment.
Compare exact, CEM, and entropy balancing on the same target.


Keep returning to the same ATT target. That prevents the methods discussion from becoming a set of disconnected technique demos.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
section: 7 What The Pre-Match Section Is Doing
text:
7 What The Pre-Match Section Is Doing

It shows the untreated group is not automatically a credible stand-in.
It reveals where the design burden is heaviest.
It tells you what kinds of costs you should expect later.

Ask:

Which pre-treatment differences worry you most?
What happens if overlap is weak on prior earnings?


Use the report’s pre-match diagnostics to make the comparison problem visible. Do not rush past this slide. It sets up why the methods sections matter.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
8 Exact Matching

strongest audit trail
easiest method to explain in plain language
often too rigid once important continuous covariates matter

Teaching line:
clear rules, but often too rigid

Stress that exact matching is valuable here as a teaching contrast. Its clarity helps, but it cannot carry the full design on its own in the lalonde example.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
9 Coarsened Exact Matching

keeps matching logic visible
brings continuous covariates into the design through bins
gives the clearest matched-sample compromise in the report

Teaching line:
auditable compromise, but dependent on cutpoints

Explain that CEM is strong precisely because its compromises stay visible. Then note that those visible cutpoints are real design choices, not neutral defaults.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
10 Entropy Balancing

preserves the original treated group
achieves very strong observed mean balance
moves more of the design cost into the weights

Teaching line:
strong balance, but cost moved into the weights

This is the moment to introduce effective sample size as the weighting analogue of sample loss. Make the cost concrete rather than abstract.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
11 What The Worked Example Shows

All three adjusted designs point in the same direction.
That directional stability does not remove the design tradeoffs.
The represented population shifts as pruning or weight concentration increases.


Push back against the lazy conclusion that positive estimates across methods settle the problem. The report’s point is that design costs still determine what the estimate means.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
12 Qualified Method Choice

Entropy balancing is strongest if preserving the original treated population is the priority.
CEM is strongest if you want an auditable matched-sample compromise.
Exact matching is easiest to teach, but not the strongest overall design here.


State this as a qualified judgement, not a universal rule. Tie each choice back to the causal question and the acceptable design cost.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
13 Discussion Prompts

What is the report’s main message in one sentence?
Which design cost worries you most in lalonde?
What would make you reject matching entirely in a real evaluation?


Use these prompts to close the loop. Participants should leave by articulating the report’s argument themselves, not just hearing you state it.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
14 If Time Runs Short
Keep only:

core report argument
pre-match diagnostics
exact vs CEM vs entropy balancing
treatment-effect summary and limitations
handoff to the next lab

Drop:

detailed code walkthroughs
robustness details
extended tuning discussion


This slide is mainly for you as facilitator. If the session slips, protect the report’s central logic and cut the implementation detail first.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
15 Handoff To The Next Materials

Teaching aid page
Main report
From Report to Labs lecture
Black Politicians lab


End with a clear next step. The room should know where to go next and what standard to carry forward: define the target, diagnose the raw comparison, then decide whether the design cost is defensible.
crumbs: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Titanic Teaching Lab: Full Code
text:
This document contains the complete executable R script for titanic_teaching_lab.qmd.
# Titanic teaching lab: executable full analysis script.
# Run with:
#   Rscript docs/labs/titanic_teaching_lab_full_code.R

options(width = 120)

# ---- Step 1: Install and load required packages ----
required_packages <- c(
  "causaldata",
  "MatchIt",
  "dplyr",
  "ggplot2"
)

missing_packages <- required_packages[!vapply(
  required_packages,
  requireNamespace,
  logical(1),
  quietly = TRUE
)]

if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  message("Installing missing packages: ", paste(missing_packages, collapse = ", "))
  install.packages(missing_packages, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

format_pct <- function(x, digits = 1) {
  ifelse(is.na(x), "-", sprintf(paste0("%.", digits, "f%%"), 100 * x))
}

ess <- function(w) {
  (sum(w)^2) / sum(w^2)
}

extract_effect <- function(model, design, treated_retained, control_ess) {
  coef_table <- coef(summary(model))
  estimate <- unname(coef_table["treat", "Estimate"])
  std_error <- unname(coef_table["treat", "Std. Error"])

  data.frame(
    design = design,
    estimate = estimate,
    std_error = std_error,
    ci_low = estimate - 1.96 * std_error,
    ci_high = estimate + 1.96 * std_error,
    treated_retained = treated_retained,
    control_ess = control_ess,
    row.names = NULL,
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}

# ---- Step 2: Load data and map variables to design roles ----
dat <- causaldata::titanic |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    treat = as.integer(sex == 0),
    outcome = as.integer(survived == 1),
    sex_f = factor(sex, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Female", "Male")),
    age_f = factor(age, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Child", "Adult")),
    class_f = factor(class, levels = 1:4, labels = c("First", "Second", "Third", "Crew"))
  )

analysis_formula <- treat ~ class_f + age_f

message("\n[Step 2] Data schema check")
dat |>
  dplyr::select(class_f, age_f, sex_f, outcome) |>
  dplyr::glimpse()

# ---- Step 3: Describe the raw comparison ----
message("\n[Step 3] Raw comparison by sex")
raw_summary <- dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(sex_f) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    survival_rate = mean(outcome),
    first_class_share = mean(class_f == "First"),
    crew_share = mean(class_f == "Crew"),
    child_share = mean(age_f == "Child"),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::rename(group = sex_f)

raw_summary_display <- raw_summary |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Group = group,
    N = n,
    `Survival rate` = format_pct(survival_rate),
    `First-class share` = format_pct(first_class_share),
    `Crew share` = format_pct(crew_share),
    `Child share` = format_pct(child_share)
  )

print(raw_summary)

female_survival_rate <- raw_summary$survival_rate[raw_summary$group == "Female"]
male_survival_rate <- raw_summary$survival_rate[raw_summary$group == "Male"]

# ---- Step 4: Inspect exact-match cells before fitting any design ----
message("\n[Step 4] Exact support cells")
all_cells <- expand.grid(
  class_f = levels(dat$class_f),
  age_f = levels(dat$age_f),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    class_f = factor(class_f, levels = levels(dat$class_f)),
    age_f = factor(age_f, levels = levels(dat$age_f))
  )

cell_support <- dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(class_f, age_f) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    women_n = sum(treat == 1L),
    men_n = sum(treat == 0L),
    women_survival = if (sum(treat == 1L) > 0) mean(outcome[treat == 1L]) else NA_real_,
    men_survival = if (sum(treat == 0L) > 0) mean(outcome[treat == 0L]) else NA_real_,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cell_support <- all_cells |>
  dplyr::left_join(cell_support, by = c("class_f", "age_f")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    women_n = dplyr::coalesce(women_n, 0L),
    men_n = dplyr::coalesce(men_n, 0L),
    women_survival = dplyr::coalesce(women_survival, NA_real_),
    men_survival = dplyr::coalesce(men_survival, NA_real_),
    gap = women_survival - men_survival,
    overlap = dplyr::case_when(
      women_n > 0 & men_n > 0 ~ "Both groups observed",
      women_n + men_n == 0 ~ "Empty cell",
      TRUE ~ "One group only"
    )
  )

cell_support_display <- cell_support |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    `Ticket class` = as.character(class_f),
    Age = as.character(age_f),
    Women = women_n,
    Men = men_n,
    `Women's survival` = format_pct(women_survival),
    `Men's survival` = format_pct(men_survival),
    `Gap (women - men)` = format_pct(gap),
    Overlap = overlap
  )

total_cells_n <- nrow(cell_support)
observed_cells_n <- sum(cell_support$women_n + cell_support$men_n > 0)
supported_cells_n <- sum(cell_support$women_n > 0 & cell_support$men_n > 0)
empty_cells <- cell_support |>
  dplyr::filter(women_n + men_n == 0) |>
  dplyr::transmute(cell = paste(class_f, "x", age_f))
empty_cells_text <- paste(empty_cells$cell, collapse = ", ")

survival_by_cell <- dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(class_f, age_f, sex_f) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    survival_rate = mean(outcome),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

support_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  survival_by_cell,
  ggplot2::aes(x = class_f, y = survival_rate, fill = sex_f)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(
    position = ggplot2::position_dodge(width = 0.78),
    width = 0.72
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_text(
    ggplot2::aes(label = n),
    position = ggplot2::position_dodge(width = 0.78),
    vjust = -0.35,
    size = 3
  ) +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~age_f) +
  ggplot2::coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1.05)) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("Female" = "#d95f02", "Male" = "#1b9e77")) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) sprintf("%.0f%%", 100 * x)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Survival can be compared within visible age-by-class cells",
    subtitle = "Numbers above bars show the number of passengers in each sex-by-cell group.",
    x = "Ticket class",
    y = "Survival rate",
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "top")

print(cell_support)
print(support_plot)

# ---- Step 5: Compare exact matching and subclassification ----
message("\n[Step 5] Exact matching and subclassification")
m_raw <- MatchIt::matchit(
  analysis_formula,
  data = dat,
  method = NULL,
  estimand = "ATT"
)

m_exact <- MatchIt::matchit(
  analysis_formula,
  data = dat,
  method = "exact",
  estimand = "ATT"
)

m_subclass <- MatchIt::matchit(
  analysis_formula,
  data = dat,
  method = "subclass",
  estimand = "ATT",
  subclass = 4
)

raw_balance_mat <- summary(m_raw, un = TRUE)$sum.all
exact_balance_mat <- summary(m_exact, un = TRUE)$sum.matched
subclass_balance_mat <- summary(m_subclass, un = TRUE)$sum.across

covariate_labels <- c(
  class_fFirst = "First class",
  class_fSecond = "Second class",
  class_fThird = "Third class",
  class_fCrew = "Crew",
  age_fChild = "Child",
  age_fAdult = "Adult"
)

balance_compare <- data.frame(
  covariate = unname(covariate_labels[rownames(exact_balance_mat)]),
  raw_smd = abs(raw_balance_mat[rownames(exact_balance_mat), "Std. Mean Diff."]),
  exact_smd = abs(exact_balance_mat[, "Std. Mean Diff."]),
  subclass_smd = abs(subclass_balance_mat[rownames(exact_balance_mat), "Std. Mean Diff."]),
  row.names = NULL,
  check.names = FALSE
)

balance_compare_display <- balance_compare |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Covariate = covariate,
    `Raw |SMD|` = sprintf("%.3f", raw_smd),
    `Exact |SMD|` = sprintf("%.3f", exact_smd),
    `Subclass |SMD|` = sprintf("%.3f", subclass_smd)
  )

print(balance_compare)

exact_data <- MatchIt::match.data(m_exact)
subclass_data <- MatchIt::match.data(m_subclass)

subclass_counts <- with(subclass_data, table(subclass, sex_f))
subclass_counts_display <- data.frame(
  Subclass = rownames(subclass_counts),
  Women = as.integer(subclass_counts[, "Female"]),
  Men = as.integer(subclass_counts[, "Male"]),
  row.names = NULL,
  check.names = FALSE
)

print(subclass_counts_display)

# ---- Step 6: Estimate the raw and adjusted contrasts ----
message("\n[Step 6] Effect comparison")
raw_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = dat)
exact_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = exact_data, weights = weights)
subclass_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = subclass_data, weights = weights)

estimate_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  extract_effect(
    raw_fit,
    design = "Raw comparison",
    treated_retained = 1,
    control_ess = sum(dat$treat == 0L)
  ),
  extract_effect(
    exact_fit,
    design = "Exact matching",
    treated_retained = sum(exact_data$treat == 1L) / sum(dat$treat == 1L),
    control_ess = ess(exact_data$weights[exact_data$treat == 0L])
  ),
  extract_effect(
    subclass_fit,
    design = "Subclassification",
    treated_retained = sum(subclass_data$treat == 1L) / sum(dat$treat == 1L),
    control_ess = ess(subclass_data$weights[subclass_data$treat == 0L])
  )
)

estimate_table_display <- estimate_table |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Design = design,
    `Estimated gap` = format_pct(estimate),
    `95% CI` = paste0(format_pct(ci_low), " to ", format_pct(ci_high)),
    `Treated retained` = format_pct(treated_retained),
    `Control ESS` = sprintf("%.1f", control_ess)
  )

print(estimate_table)

raw_gap_pp <- 100 * estimate_table$estimate[estimate_table$design == "Raw comparison"]
exact_gap_pp <- 100 * estimate_table$estimate[estimate_table$design == "Exact matching"]
subclass_gap_pp <- 100 * estimate_table$estimate[estimate_table$design == "Subclassification"]

message("\nDone: Titanic teaching lab workflow executed.")

Observational sample is NSW treated + CPS controls (...R:35-39)
Matches the benchmarking design used in Mixtape-style comparisons.
Pass
None


N4
Raw observational estimate is reported before any adjustment (...R:89-100, ...R:242)
Required baseline to show observational bias before design-stage adjustment.
Pass
None


N5
Baseline imbalance is diagnosed with matchit(..., method = NULL) and bal.tab (...R:103-111)
Consistent with design-first diagnostics used elsewhere in this repository.
Pass
None


N6
Exact matching branch uses a discrete formula and adds continuous diagnostics (...R:157-179)
Reasonable transparency-first branch, but not part of canonical NSW/CPS benchmark scripts.
Partial alignment
Keep as training extension and label as supplemental branch.


N7
CEM branch includes age/earnings coarsening via documented cutpoints (...R:182-204)
Aligned with the objective of reducing severe imbalance while preserving an ATT focus.
Pass
None


N8
Entropy balancing branch includes first and second moments for continuous covariates (...R:212-221)
Aligned with standard entropy-balancing practice for strong mean-moment balance.
Pass
None


N9
Weight diagnostics report ESS and maximum weight (...R:229-241)
Appropriate for checking weight concentration risk in benchmark recovery.
Pass
None


N10
Final output reports benchmark_gap and abs_benchmark_gap for each method (...R:271-277)
Directly implements the lab’s core benchmark criterion.
Pass
None

title: Quality Assurance Log: NSW + CPS Benchmark Evaluated Code
text:
5 QA Decision
The evaluated NSW/CPS benchmark code is ready for training use.
It reproduces the core benchmark logic correctly and exposes method tradeoffs (balance, retention, and benchmark gap) in a way that is consistent with the repository’s design-first style.
Two branches (exact matching and explicit propensity-overlap summaries) are intentional pedagogic extensions beyond a strict minimal NSW/CPS benchmark script and should remain labeled as such.

title: Quality Assurance Log: NSW + CPS Benchmark Evaluated Code
section: 6 Optional Tightening If Strict Replication Is Needed
text:
6 Optional Tightening If Strict Replication Is Needed

Add a “minimal benchmark mode” that keeps only raw, CEM, and entropy balancing branches.
Freeze a single CEM cutpoint specification and report robustness variants separately.
Add a fixed tolerance rule for benchmark closeness (for example, absolute gap under a pre-specified threshold) for pass/fail reporting.

title: NSW + CPS Benchmark: Evaluated Code
text: This page runs the evaluated workflow script and shows processed benchmark outputs.

title: NSW + CPS Benchmark: Evaluated Code
text:
1 Evaluated Script
# NSW + CPS benchmark lab: evaluated code with processed outputs.
# Run with:
#   Rscript docs/labs/nsw_cps_benchmark_lab_evaluated_code.R

options(width = 120)

# ---- Step 1: Install and load required packages ----
required_packages <- c(
  "causaldata",
  "MatchIt",
  "WeightIt",
  "cobalt",
  "dplyr",
  "ggplot2"
)

missing_packages <- required_packages[!vapply(
  required_packages,
  requireNamespace,
  logical(1),
  quietly = TRUE
)]

if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  message("Installing missing packages: ", paste(missing_packages, collapse = ", "))
  install.packages(missing_packages, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

# ---- Step 2: Build benchmark and observational datasets ----
nsw <- causaldata::nsw_mixtape |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    treat = as.integer(treat),
    outcome = re78
  )

cps <- causaldata::cps_mixtape |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    treat = as.integer(treat),
    outcome = re78
  )

benchmark_dat <- nsw

obs_dat <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  nsw |>
    dplyr::filter(treat == 1L) |>
    dplyr::mutate(source = "NSW treated"),
  cps |>
    dplyr::filter(treat == 0L) |>
    dplyr::mutate(source = "CPS controls")
)

design_covariates <- c("age", "educ", "black", "hisp", "marr", "nodegree", "re74", "re75")

dat_preview <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::select(source, treat, outcome, dplyr::all_of(design_covariates)) |>
  head(10)

# ---- Step 3: Experimental benchmark ----
benchmark_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = benchmark_dat)
benchmark_att <- coef(summary(benchmark_fit))["treat", ]
experimental_estimate <- unname(benchmark_att["Estimate"])

benchmark_summary <- benchmark_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    mean_re78 = mean(outcome, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re74 = mean(re74, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re75 = mean(re75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "NSW treated", "NSW controls")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, n, mean_re78, mean_re74, mean_re75)

benchmark_effect <- data.frame(
  design = "Experimental benchmark (NSW RCT)",
  estimate = unname(benchmark_att["Estimate"]),
  std_error = unname(benchmark_att["Std. Error"]),
  t_value = unname(benchmark_att["t value"]),
  p_value = unname(benchmark_att["Pr(>|t|)"]),
  row.names = NULL,
  check.names = FALSE
)

# ---- Step 4: Raw observational comparison ----
raw_summary <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    mean_re78 = mean(outcome, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_educ = mean(educ, na.rm = TRUE),
    prop_black = mean(black == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re74 = mean(re74, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re75 = mean(re75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "NSW treated", "CPS controls")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, n, mean_re78, mean_age, mean_educ, prop_black, mean_re74, mean_re75)

raw_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = obs_dat)

# ---- Step 5: Baseline balance and overlap diagnostics ----
m_raw <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ age + educ + black + hisp + marr + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data = obs_dat,
  method = NULL,
  estimand = "ATT"
)

raw_balance <- cobalt::bal.tab(m_raw, un = TRUE, binary = "std", m.threshold = 0.1)

ps_fit <- stats::glm(
  treat ~ age + educ + black + hisp + marr + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data = obs_dat,
  family = stats::binomial()
)

obs_dat <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::mutate(ps = predict(ps_fit, type = "response"))

overlap_bounds <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    min_ps = min(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_ps = max(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

common_support_low <- max(overlap_bounds$min_ps)
common_support_high <- min(overlap_bounds$max_ps)

overlap_summary <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    min_ps = min(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    p10_ps = as.numeric(stats::quantile(ps, 0.10, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p50_ps = as.numeric(stats::quantile(ps, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p90_ps = as.numeric(stats::quantile(ps, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE)),
    max_ps = max(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    in_common_support = mean(ps >= common_support_low & ps <= common_support_high),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "NSW treated", "CPS controls")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, n, min_ps, p10_ps, p50_ps, p90_ps, max_ps, in_common_support)

# ---- Step 6: Exact matching ----
m_exact <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ black + hisp + marr + nodegree + educ,
  data = obs_dat,
  method = "exact",
  estimand = "ATT"
)

exact_dat <- MatchIt::match.data(m_exact)
exact_counts <- exact_dat |>
  dplyr::count(treat) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control"))

exact_balance <- cobalt::bal.tab(
  m_exact,
  data = obs_dat,
  un = TRUE,
  binary = "std",
  m.threshold = 0.1,
  addl = ~ age + re74 + re75
)

# ---- Step 7: CEM ----
cem_cutpoints <- list(
  age = "q5",
  educ = 4,
  re74 = "q6",
  re75 = "q6"
)

m_cem <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ age + educ + black + hisp + marr + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data = obs_dat,
  method = "cem",
  estimand = "ATT",
  cutpoints = cem_cutpoints
)

cem_dat <- MatchIt::match.data(m_cem)
cem_counts <- cem_dat |>
  dplyr::count(treat) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control"))

cem_balance <- cobalt::bal.tab(m_cem, un = TRUE, binary = "std", m.threshold = 0.1)

# ---- Step 8: Entropy balancing ----
w_ebal <- WeightIt::weightit(
  treat ~ age + I(age^2) +
    educ + I(educ^2) +
    black + hisp + marr + nodegree +
    re74 + I(re74^2) +
    re75 + I(re75^2),
  data = obs_dat,
  method = "ebal",
  estimand = "ATT"
)

ebal_balance <- cobalt::bal.tab(w_ebal, un = TRUE, binary = "std", m.threshold = 0.1)

ess <- function(w) {
  (sum(w)^2) / sum(w^2)
}

obs_ebal <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::mutate(w = w_ebal$weights)

ebal_weight_summary <- obs_ebal |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    raw_n = dplyr::n(),
    ess = ess(w),
    max_weight = max(w),
    mean_weight = mean(w),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, raw_n, ess, max_weight, mean_weight)

# ---- Step 9: Effect estimates and benchmark gaps ----
exact_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = exact_dat, weights = weights)
cem_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = cem_dat, weights = weights)
ebal_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = obs_ebal, weights = w)

get_treat_row <- function(fit, design_name) {
  out <- coef(summary(fit))
  data.frame(
    design = design_name,
    estimate = unname(out["treat", "Estimate"]),
    std_error = unname(out["treat", "Std. Error"]),
    t_value = unname(out["treat", "t value"]),
    p_value = unname(out["treat", "Pr(>|t|)"]),
    row.names = NULL,
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}

estimate_comparison <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  benchmark_effect,
  get_treat_row(raw_fit, "Raw observational (NSW treated vs CPS)"),
  get_treat_row(exact_fit, "Exact matching"),
  get_treat_row(cem_fit, "CEM"),
  get_treat_row(ebal_fit, "Entropy balancing")
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    benchmark_gap = estimate - experimental_estimate,
    abs_benchmark_gap = abs(benchmark_gap)
  )

method_retention <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  data.frame(design = "Raw observational", treated_retained = sum(obs_dat$treat == 1L), control_retained = sum(obs_dat$treat == 0L)),
  data.frame(design = "Exact matching", treated_retained = sum(exact_dat$treat == 1L), control_retained = sum(exact_dat$treat == 0L)),
  data.frame(design = "CEM", treated_retained = sum(cem_dat$treat == 1L), control_retained = sum(cem_dat$treat == 0L)),
  data.frame(design = "Entropy balancing", treated_retained = sum(obs_ebal$treat == 1L), control_retained = sum(obs_ebal$treat == 0L))
)

benchmark_gap_rank <- estimate_comparison |>
  dplyr::filter(design != "Experimental benchmark (NSW RCT)") |>
  dplyr::arrange(abs_benchmark_gap) |>
  dplyr::select(design, estimate, benchmark_gap, abs_benchmark_gap)

# ---- Step 10: Print processed objects for non-interactive runs ----
message("\n[Processed data preview]")
print(dat_preview)

message("\n[Experimental benchmark summary]")
print(benchmark_summary)

message("\n[Raw observational summary]")
print(raw_summary)

message("\n[Overlap summary]")
print(overlap_summary)

message("\n[Exact matched counts]")
print(exact_counts)

message("\n[CEM matched counts]")
print(cem_counts)

message("\n[Entropy balancing weight summary]")
print(ebal_weight_summary)

message("\n[Method retention]")
print(method_retention)

message("\n[Estimate comparison]")
print(estimate_comparison)

message("\n[Benchmark gap ranking]")
print(benchmark_gap_rank)

message("\nDone: evaluated NSW + CPS benchmark workflow objects created.")

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
Use this page as a facilitator script for a 60 minute teaching lab built around the main lalonde report.

It is designed for live delivery, not as a standalone methods article.
It assumes participants have the main report open.
It keeps the session focused on the report’s main messages rather than on running every line of code.







Note



This page is deliberately non-executed. The goal is delivery support: timing, prompts, transitions, and decisions about what to emphasize in the room.
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
Use this page as a facilitator script for a 60 minute teaching lab built around the main lalonde report.

It is designed for live delivery, not as a standalone methods article.
It assumes participants have the main report open.
It keeps the session focused on the report’s main messages rather than on running every line of code.







Note



This page is deliberately non-executed. The goal is delivery support: timing, prompts, transitions, and decisions about what to emphasize in the room.
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
2 Session Goal
By the end of the hour, participants should be able to explain the report’s main claim in plain language:
Matching and weighting methods are useful design tools, but they should be judged by the tradeoff they impose between balance, overlap, retention, and weight concentration rather than by any single balance table or treatment estimate.
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
3 Learning Objectives
At the end of the session, participants should be able to:

state why the report starts with the estimand rather than the method
explain the difference between matching and weighting as design strategies
interpret exact matching, CEM, and entropy balancing as different solutions to different design problems
describe why acceptable balance alone is not enough
defend a qualified method choice for the lalonde example
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
4 Recommended Setup
Keep these pages open during delivery:

Main report
Main report full code
Matching and Weighting training deck
From Report to Labs lecture deck
Black Politicians lab

If you are presenting live:

use the slide deck for opening framing
switch to the main report for the worked example sections
use this teaching aid as your pacing and prompt sheet
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
5 60-Minute Run Of Show








Time
Segment
Delivery aim




0-5 min
Opening frame
Set the causal-design lens and tell participants what to listen for


5-15 min
Core report argument
Land the report’s three main messages before touching methods


15-25 min
Design-first workflow
Walk through estimand, covariates, raw imbalance, and overlap


25-40 min
Method comparison
Contrast exact matching, CEM, and entropy balancing on the same ATT target


40-50 min
Interpreting the worked example
Explain what the lalonde results do and do not justify


50-60 min
Discussion and handoff
Check understanding and transition into the lab sequence
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
6 Delivery Script By Segment

6.1 0-5 Minutes: Opening Frame
Main message to deliver:

This is a lab about design judgement, not software button-pushing.
The report compares methods that solve related but not identical problems.
The central question is not “which method wins?” but “what design cost are we willing to pay?”

Suggested wording:

Today’s report matters because it shows that matching and weighting are not magic corrections for observational data. They are design tools that make the comparison problem more visible. Our job in this lab is to learn how to read those design costs clearly.

Ask the room:

What usually gets reported first in applied matching write-ups: the design or the estimate?
Why might that be a problem?



6.2 5-15 Minutes: Core Report Argument
Point participants to:

Executive Summary
Introduction
Design Principles

Main messages to land:

Start with the causal question and target estimand.
Matching and weighting are design-stage tools, not substitutes for assumptions.
Balance has to be read together with retained information.

Facilitator prompt:

Ask one participant to summarize the report’s argument in one sentence.
If they only mention balance, push them to add retention or effective sample size.

Checkpoint sentence:

A good design is not the one with the prettiest post-adjustment table. It is the one whose tradeoffs still match the causal question.



6.3 15-25 Minutes: Design-First Workflow
Point participants to:

Define the Estimand Before Adjustment
Data and Study Design
Pre-Match Diagnostics

Walk through this sequence:

identify the target as the ATT
define the treated group as programme participants
name the pre-treatment covariates
inspect the raw comparison before any adjustment

Main delivery point:

Participants should see that the untreated group is not automatically a credible stand-in.
The pre-match section matters because it shows the burden the design has to carry.

Ask the room:

Which covariates look most substantively worrying before adjustment?
If overlap is poor on prior earnings, what kinds of costs should we expect later?



6.4 25-40 Minutes: Method Comparison
Point participants to:

Exact Matching
Coarsened Exact Matching
Entropy Balancing for Weighting
Comparing the Methods

Use this structure:








Method
What to emphasize
What to warn about




Exact matching
Most transparent and auditable logic
Can force a reduced covariate set and still leave important imbalance


CEM
Keeps matching logic visible while bringing continuous covariates into the design
Coarsening rules change who is retained


Entropy balancing
Preserves treated units and achieves very strong observed mean balance
Balance can be purchased with concentrated weights and lower control ESS



Teaching line for each method:

Exact matching: “clear rules, but often too rigid”
CEM: “auditable compromise, but dependent on cutpoints”
Entropy balancing: “strong balance, but cost moved into the weights”

Facilitator move:

Keep bringing the comparison back to the same ATT.
Do not let the discussion drift into treating each method as if it were answering a different question by default.



6.5 40-50 Minutes: Interpreting The Worked Example
Point participants to:

Treatment Effect Summary
Limitations
Conclusion

Main points to land:

All three adjusted designs give a positive estimated effect in direction.
That directional stability does not remove the design tradeoffs.
The represented population changes as pruning or weight concentration increases.

Suggested summary:

In this report, entropy balancing is the strongest summary design if preserving the original treated population is the priority. CEM is the clearest matched-sample compromise if auditability and explicit sample restriction matter most. Exact matching is the easiest to explain, but it is not the strongest overall design here.

Ask the room:

Why is it wrong to stop at “all three methods give a positive result”?
Which method would you defend to a policy audience, and what cost would you have to admit?



6.6 50-60 Minutes: Discussion And Handoff
Use the last ten minutes to consolidate rather than add new content.
Ask participants to answer:

What is the report’s main message in one sentence?
Which design cost worries you most in the lalonde example?
What would make you reject matching entirely in a real evaluation?

Then hand off to:

Black Politicians lab if you want a second design-first comparison
NSW + CPS benchmark lab if you want an experimental benchmark
Titanic teaching lab if you want a compact follow-up
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
section: 7 The Three Messages To Repeat Throughout
text:
7 The Three Messages To Repeat Throughout
If time is short, make sure these three messages are repeated clearly:

define the estimand before choosing the adjustment method
read balance together with overlap, retention, and weight concentration
treat adjusted estimates as design-contingent, not mechanically correct
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
8 Common Teaching Risks

8.1 Risk 1: The Session Becomes A Software Demo
Correction:

Move back to the report prose before returning to code output.
Ask what causal question the current diagnostic is answering.



8.2 Risk 2: Participants Assume Better Balance Means Better Inference
Correction:

Bring in retention and effective sample size immediately.
Ask who the adjusted estimate still describes.



8.3 Risk 3: Discussion Gets Stuck On Which Method Is “Best”
Correction:

Reframe around the causal question.
Ask what tradeoff each method imposes and whether that tradeoff is acceptable.
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
9 Quick Facilitation Prompts
Use these when discussion slows down:

What changed between the raw sample and the adjusted design?
Which method keeps the clearest audit trail?
Which method hides more of its cost in weights rather than dropped units?
If you had to explain this to a non-technical commissioner, what would you say in two sentences?
What evidence would you want before trusting the estimate more strongly?
crumbs: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
section: 10 Minimal Version If Time Runs Short
text:
10 Minimal Version If Time Runs Short
If you lose 10-15 minutes, keep only this sequence:

executive summary and design principles
pre-match diagnostics

title: Teaching Aid: Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab
text:
11 Suggested Closing
Close with a sentence that keeps the report’s tone:

The value of matching and weighting is not that they manufacture certainty. It is that they force us to say, clearly and publicly, what comparison we are willing to defend and what cost we paid to make it plausible.

title: Black Politicians Training Aid: Evaluated Code
text: This page runs the evaluated workflow script and shows processed data objects created during execution.

title: Black Politicians Training Aid: Evaluated Code
text:
1 Evaluated Script
# Black Politicians training aid: evaluated code with processed outputs.
# Run with:
#   Rscript docs/labs/black_politicians_lab_evaluated_code.R

options(width = 120)

# ---- Step 1: Install and load required packages ----
required_packages <- c(
  "causaldata",
  "MatchIt",
  "WeightIt",
  "cobalt",
  "dplyr",
  "ggplot2"
)

missing_packages <- required_packages[!vapply(
  required_packages,
  requireNamespace,
  logical(1),
  quietly = TRUE
)]

if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  message("Installing missing packages: ", paste(missing_packages, collapse = ", "))
  install.packages(missing_packages, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

# ---- Step 2: Build analysis dataset used by all methods ----
dat <- causaldata::black_politicians |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    treat = as.integer(leg_black),
    outcome = as.integer(responded)
  )

design_covariates <- c("medianhhincom", "blackpercent", "leg_democrat")

dat_preview <- dat |>
  dplyr::select(treat, outcome, treat_out, dplyr::all_of(design_covariates)) |>
  head(10)

# ---- Step 3: Raw comparison summary ----
raw_summary <- dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    response_rate = mean(outcome, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_income = mean(medianhhincom, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_blackpercent = mean(blackpercent, na.rm = TRUE),
    prop_democrat = mean(leg_democrat == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Black legislators", "Non-Black legislators")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, n, response_rate, mean_income, mean_blackpercent, prop_democrat)

# ---- Step 4: Baseline balance benchmark ----
m_raw <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ medianhhincom + blackpercent + leg_democrat,
  data = dat,
  method = NULL,
  estimand = "ATT"
)

raw_balance <- cobalt::bal.tab(m_raw, un = TRUE, m.threshold = 0.1)

# ---- Step 5: Exact matching ----
m_exact <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ leg_democrat + leg_senator + south,
  data = dat,
  method = "exact",
  estimand = "ATT"
)

exact_dat <- MatchIt::match.data(m_exact)
exact_counts <- exact_dat |>
  dplyr::count(treat) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control"))

# ---- Step 6: CEM ----
create_even_breaks <- function(x, n) {
  min_x <- min(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  max_x <- max(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  min_x + ((0:n) / n) * (max_x - min_x)
}

cem_cutpoints <- list(
  medianhhincom = quantile(dat$medianhhincom, probs = (0:6) / 6, na.rm = TRUE),
  blackpercent = create_even_breaks(dat$blackpercent, 6)
)

m_cem <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ medianhhincom + blackpercent + leg_democrat + leg_senator + south,
  data = dat,
  method = "cem",
  estimand = "ATT",
  cutpoints = cem_cutpoints
)

cem_dat <- MatchIt::match.data(m_cem)
cem_counts <- cem_dat |>
  dplyr::count(treat) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control"))

cem_balance <- cobalt::bal.tab(m_cem, un = TRUE, m.threshold = 0.1)

# ---- Step 7: Entropy balancing and ESS diagnostics ----
w_ebal <- WeightIt::weightit(
  treat ~ medianhhincom + I(medianhhincom^2) +
    blackpercent + I(blackpercent^2) +
    leg_democrat + leg_senator + south,
  data = dat,
  method = "ebal",
  estimand = "ATT"
)

ebal_balance <- cobalt::bal.tab(w_ebal, un = TRUE, m.threshold = 0.1)

ess <- function(w) {
  (sum(w)^2) / sum(w^2)
}

dat_ebal <- dat |>
  dplyr::mutate(w = w_ebal$weights)

ebal_weight_summary <- dat_ebal |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    raw_n = dplyr::n(),
    ess = ess(w),
    max_weight = max(w),
    mean_weight = mean(w),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, raw_n, ess, max_weight, mean_weight)

# ---- Step 8: Outcome models across designs ----
raw_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = dat)
exact_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = exact_dat, weights = weights)
cem_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = cem_dat, weights = weights)
ebal_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = dat_ebal, weights = w)

get_treat_row <- function(fit, design_name) {
  out <- coef(summary(fit))
  data.frame(
    design = design_name,
    estimate = unname(out["treat", "Estimate"]),
    std_error = unname(out["treat", "Std. Error"]),
    t_value = unname(out["treat", "t value"]),
    p_value = unname(out["treat", "Pr(>|t|)"]),
    row.names = NULL,
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}

estimate_comparison <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  get_treat_row(raw_fit, "Raw sample"),
  get_treat_row(exact_fit, "Exact matching"),
  get_treat_row(cem_fit, "CEM"),
  get_treat_row(ebal_fit, "Entropy balancing")
)

# ---- Step 8b: Book-style weighted interaction model using CEM weights ----
# This mirrors the chapter's richer specification after the design stage.
final_fit <- stats::lm(
  outcome ~ treat * treat_out +
    nonblacknonwhite +
    black_medianhh +
    white_medianhh +
    statessquireindex +
    totalpop +
    urbanpercent,
  data = cem_dat,
  weights = weights
)

final_fit_coef <- coef(summary(final_fit))
final_fit_key_terms <- data.frame(
  term = rownames(final_fit_coef),
  estimate = unname(final_fit_coef[, "Estimate"]),
  std_error = unname(final_fit_coef[, "Std. Error"]),
  t_value = unname(final_fit_coef[, "t value"]),
  p_value = unname(final_fit_coef[, "Pr(>|t|)"]),
  row.names = NULL,
  check.names = FALSE
)

final_fit_key_terms <- final_fit_key_terms[
  final_fit_key_terms$term %in% c("treat", "treat_out", "treat:treat_out"),
]

# ---- Step 9: Print processed data objects for non-interactive runs ----
message("\n[Processed data preview]")
print(dat_preview)

message("\n[Raw summary]")
print(raw_summary)

message("\n[Exact matched counts]")
print(exact_counts)

message("\n[CEM matched counts]")
print(cem_counts)

message("\n[Entropy balancing weight summary]")
print(ebal_weight_summary)

message("\n[Effect estimate comparison]")
print(estimate_comparison)

message("\n[Book-replication interaction model key terms]")
print(final_fit_key_terms)

message("\nDone: evaluated workflow objects created.")

title: Quality Assurance Log: Matching Methods Report
text:
4 QA Decision
The report is documentation-aligned with MatchIt 4.7.2 and WeightIt 1.5.1 for the checked implementation and interpretation claims.

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
This report compares exact matching, coarsened exact matching (CEM), and entropy balancing as design-stage tools for estimating an ATT from observational data.
In the lalonde worked example, all three adjusted designs produce a positive programme-effect estimate, but they do so with different tradeoffs in transparency, covariate balance, and retained information.
Exact matching is the most transparent design, but it remains a partial solution here because feasible exact constraints leave residual imbalance on prior earnings.
The report’s tuned CEM specification is the strongest matched-sample compromise: it brings all reported covariates below the conventional absolute standardized-difference threshold of 0.1, but it does so by pruning the treated sample from 185 units to 81.
Entropy balancing achieves the strongest observed mean balance and preserves all treated units, but the weighted comparison depends on a much smaller effective control sample, so the gain in balance comes with concentrated weights rather than explicit sample loss.
For evaluator practice, the main implication is that method choice should follow the causal question and the available overlap. In this example, entropy balancing is the strongest summary design when preserving the original treated population is the priority, while CEM is the clearest option when an auditable matched sample matters most.

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
