Delivering the Main Report in a 1-Hour Lab

title: Slides
text:
2 Suggested Slide Pipeline

Keep deck narrative aligned with report/matching_methods_report.qmd.
Use the main report deck first, then the background lecture deck before opening the labs.
Use the 1-hour lab deck when you want a tighter facilitator-led session built around the teaching aid.
Pair the main report example with one lab extension at a time.
Link slides back to full-code, evaluated-code, and QA outputs so the deck stays auditable.
Export speaker-ready slides from Quarto RevealJS.

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
Start with the causal question and estimand, not the method.
Judge balance together with overlap, retention, and weight concentration.
Exact matching, CEM, and entropy balancing solve different design problems.


Open with the report’s core claim: there is no default winner across methods. The audience should leave with a decision rule, not a favorite algorithm. Emphasize that the comparison is about design tradeoffs, not software output.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
Start with the causal question and estimand, not the method.
Judge balance together with overlap, retention, and weight concentration.
Exact matching, CEM, and entropy balancing solve different design problems.


Open with the report’s core claim: there is no default winner across methods. The audience should leave with a decision rule, not a favorite algorithm. Emphasize that the comparison is about design tradeoffs, not software output.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
2 Why This Comparison Matters

These are design-stage tools for making treated and untreated units more comparable before outcome analysis.
They improve transparency more reliably than they guarantee validity.
A balanced design can still be weak if overlap is poor or key confounders are unobserved.


This slide aligns with the report’s early framing. Stress that matching and weighting are useful because they force explicit decisions about comparability. Also make the limit explicit: none of these methods solves unmeasured confounding.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
3 Report Workflow

Define the estimand and focal population.
Choose defensible pre-treatment covariates.
Diagnose raw imbalance and overlap.
Compare methods on the same target.
Estimate effects only after the design is credible.


Walk through this as the report’s organizing logic. If the audience remembers one process point, it should be that outcome modeling comes after design diagnostics, not before them.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
4 The Core Tradeoff
You usually cannot maximize all three at once:

transparent comparison rules
strong observed balance
preservation of the original treated population

That tradeoff is the report’s main theme.

This is the design triangle from the report in plain language. Set up the rest of the deck by saying each method sits in a different part of this tradeoff space.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
5 Main Worked Example
The main article uses MatchIt::lalonde to estimate an ATT.

Focal group: programme participants
Pre-treatment covariates include age, education, race, marital status, degree status, and prior earnings
Teaching goal: compare balance gains against information loss

Main report

Keep this brief. The purpose is not to reteach the whole dataset, but to remind the audience that the report’s substantive question is the earnings effect for the treated group.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
6 What The Main Report Finds








Method
Main strength
Main cost




Exact matching
Most auditable design
Residual imbalance on prior earnings


CEM
Clearest matched-sample compromise
Treated sample shrinks from 185 to 81


Entropy balancing
Preserves all treated units
Effective control sample falls to about 98.5




Present this as the report’s headline comparison. Use the numbers to make the cost concrete. Do not oversell any one method: exact matching is transparent, CEM is the strongest matched-sample compromise, and entropy balancing is strongest when preserving the treated population is the priority.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
7 Main Report Decision Rule

Use exact matching when the design is genuinely low-dimensional and auditability matters most.
Use CEM when you want an inspectable matched sample across mixed covariate types.
Use entropy balancing when retaining the original treated population matters most and the weights stay credible.


This slide translates the report into evaluator guidance. Say explicitly that method choice follows the question and overlap conditions, not analyst preference.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
8 Why Add A Second Dataset
The Black Politicians lab tests whether the same logic holds outside the lalonde example.

Data: causaldata::black_politicians
Training comparison: leg_black
Outcome: responded
Shared target: ATT

Lab page

Frame this as a stress test of the report’s narrative. The point is not that leg_black is a policy treatment; the point is to teach comparability, overlap, and estimand drift on a second dataset.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
9 Black Politicians: Design Problem

Raw response rate is 0.393 for Black legislators versus 0.425 for non-Black legislators.
Mean district Black share is 0.517 versus 0.063.
Democratic share is 0.978 versus 0.501.
Raw max absolute SMD across the audited covariates is 2.247.

This is a structural comparability problem, not a small baseline mismatch.

Use this slide to slow down and show why the design burden is real. The audience should see that raw group differences are substantial before any method is applied.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
10 Black Politicians: Exact Matching

Best used here as a feasibility check, not the preferred final design.
It retains all 364 treated units on the chosen discrete covariates.
It still leaves open the question of continuous comparability on district composition and resources.


Connect this back to the report’s exact matching discussion. Retention alone is not evidence of a good design. Explain that exact agreement on a few discrete variables can still miss large continuous differences.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
11 Black Politicians: CEM

CEM keeps the matching logic visible while handling continuous covariates through bins.
Max adjusted absolute SMD falls to 0.093.
The treated sample falls from 364 to 300.

This is the lab’s clearest matched-sample compromise.

Make the parallel to the main report explicit: CEM again plays the role of the most legible matched-sample design. Note that the cost is visible sample pruning, which shifts the practical target toward treated units with support.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
12 Black Politicians: Entropy Balancing

Included adjusted mean differences are driven to 0.000.
The control ESS falls to about 74.8.
The maximum control weight rises to about 209.

The balance table improves, but the weighted comparison becomes fragile.

This is the same caution as in the report, but sharper. Tell the audience that entropy balancing solves the observed mean-balance problem while moving the design cost into a much more concentrated weighted comparison.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
13 What Changes Across Designs

The simple treat estimate is not stable across raw, exact, CEM, and entropy-balanced designs.
That instability is the point of the exercise, not a nuisance.
Design diagnostics need to be interpreted before any substantive effect claim.

Evaluated lab output

Use this slide to explain why the report separates design from effect interpretation. If the estimate moves materially when the comparison group changes, the design choice is doing real work and must be defended.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
14 Practical Evaluator Takeaway

Matching and weighting are useful when rich pre-treatment covariates are available and stronger quasi-experimental designs are not.
Better balance is not enough if the design no longer represents the intended ATT.
The right question is not “which method wins?” but “which cost is acceptable for this causal question?”


Bring the deck back to the report’s evaluator framing. Mention that matching should sit inside a wider quasi-experimental toolkit rather than act as the default response to observational data.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
15 Read / Run / Audit

Main report
Main report full code
Black Politicians lab
Black Politicians evaluated code
Black Politicians QA log


Close by pointing the audience to the materials that support the claims in the slides. If presenting live, mention which page to use for teaching delivery and which page to use for code auditing.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting Learning Resources
text:
This website is a training and reference space for design-stage causal inference using matching and weighting methods in observational evaluation.
Its core goal is to show how to design, diagnose, and estimate observational comparisons responsibly using:

exact matching
coarsened exact matching
entropy balancing for weighting

The material is organized around a practical evaluator workflow rather than a software-first workflow. The emphasis throughout the site is that:

matching and weighting are design tools, not substitutes for causal assumptions
balance must be assessed together with retained sample size and overlap
entropy balancing should be treated as a weighting method, not as a matching method

The intended audience is evaluators, analysts, and staff who need a practical introduction to quasi-experimental design choices in programme and policy evaluation. The framing is especially aligned with training and capacity-building use cases, where methods need to be technically sound, explainable, and auditable.

title: Matching and Weighting Learning Resources
text:
2 How The Site Is Structured
The site is split into three main areas:

Reports: longer methods write-ups and their supporting audit materials
Additional Labs: worked examples that extend the core material to other datasets and teaching scenarios
Slides: presentation decks that support training delivery and lab handoff

If you are new to the site, the recommended sequence is:

Start with the main report.
Use the full-code and QA pages when you want implementation detail or audit traceability.
Move into the labs for additional worked examples.
Use the slide decks when you want a delivery-ready teaching version.

title: Matching and Weighting Learning Resources
text:
3 Reports
The main report bundle is:

Matching Methods Report
Synthesis Report: Main Report versus The Mixtape, The Effect, and Gertler Chapter 8
Propensity-Score Weighting Companion
Notes on The Effect: Matching
Notes on The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
Full Code Companion
QA Log

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
These are study notes on The Effect, Chapter 14 (“Matching”). They are meant to help with understanding and recall, not to reproduce the chapter. The structure follows the chapter so that each section can be read alongside the source.
The aim here is to capture:

the main idea of each section
why the section matters for causal inference
what conceptual traps to watch for
how the chapter connects to this repo’s design-first framing

the main idea of each section
why the section matters for causal inference
what conceptual traps to watch for
how the chapter connects to this repo’s design-first framing

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
2 Recommended Report Shape
The current repo pattern suggests using a three-layer structure:

A short executive layer that states what the chapter is for and how it fits this project.
A section-by-section notes layer that mirrors the source chapter.
A synthesis layer that translates the chapter into repo actions, extensions, and open questions.

That yields the following scaffold:

Executive Summary
Source Baseline
Chapter Structure and Notes
Implications for This Repo
Follow-Up Build Plan

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
2 Source Baseline

Source: The Effect, Chapter 14 - Matching
Author: Nick Huntington-Klein
Accessed for these notes: 7 March 2026
Current chapter sections observed in the live rendering:

14.1 Another Way to Close Back Doors
14.2 Weighted Averages
14.3 Matching in Concept: A Single Matching Variable
14.4 Multiple Matching Variables
14.5 Propensity Score Weighting with Multiple Matching Variables
14.6 Assumptions for Matching
14.7 Estimation with Matched Data
14.8 Matching and Treatment Effects

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
3 Executive Summary

The chapter presents matching as one way to make treated and untreated groups more comparable when treatment was not randomly assigned.
A core theme is that matching is easier to understand if it is seen as a weighting and design problem rather than as a mysterious algorithm.
The chapter gradually shifts from intuition to practical implementation: first why matching might help, then how weights arise, then how multivariate matching works, then what assumptions still remain.
The most important takeaway is that matching does not “create” causal identification by itself. It can make observed groups more comparable, but only under strong assumptions about observed confounding, overlap, and acceptable balance.
For this repo, the chapter is especially useful because it aligns with the argument that design comes before estimation and that diagnostics must be taken seriously.

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
4 Chapter Structure and Notes

4.1 14.1 Another Way to Close Back Doors
Main idea:

The chapter introduces matching as another strategy for closing back-door paths. Instead of using regression to adjust for confounders parametrically, the analyst tries to compare treated and untreated units that are similar on observed covariates.

Why this matters:

This is a useful reframing because it reminds the reader that causal identification is about comparability, not about any one model class.
It also helps explain why matching often feels more intuitive to applied researchers: “find comparable cases” is easier to picture than “correctly specify a regression surface.”

Key understanding points:

Matching is still trying to address confounding, not avoid it.
The basic promise of matching is design transparency: the comparison is made more explicit.
The basic risk is that matching can look credible even when key confounders are missing or overlap is weak.

What to remember:

Regression and matching are not enemies here; they are different tools for the same underlying causal problem.
The chapter uses matching to teach causal design logic, not to argue that regression is obsolete.

Useful caution:

It is easy to overstate what matching buys you. Matching can improve observed comparability, but if the important back doors are not measured, the core problem remains.



4.2 14.2 Weighted Averages
Main idea:

The chapter emphasizes that many matching procedures can be understood in terms of weighted averages. Some observations get more influence, some get less, and some may effectively drop out.

Why this matters:

This is a strong conceptual move because it unifies several methods that otherwise seem unrelated.
Once matching is understood as a way of constructing weights, later sections on entropy balancing and weighted estimation become much easier to follow.

Key understanding points:

A matched analysis is often not just “keep these rows and drop those rows.”
Even nearest-neighbor or subclassification approaches often imply weights.
Thinking in weights clarifies what population is being represented after matching.

What to remember:

Weighting is not just a separate method family; it is also a language for understanding matching outputs.
This section helps prevent the common mistake of treating matched data as if the design stage were conceptually disconnected from the estimation stage.

Useful caution:

If one small set of units carries very large implied weight, apparent balance may hide fragility. The weighted-average framing makes this easier to see.



4.3 14.3 Matching in Concept: A Single Matching Variable
Main idea:

The chapter first explains matching in a simple one-variable case. This strips away the multivariate complexity and lets the reader focus on what it means to find “close” controls for treated units.

Why this matters:

This is where the intuition for distance, closeness, and tradeoffs becomes concrete.
A one-variable example helps explain why exact matches are sometimes possible, why approximate matches may be needed, and why bad matches can still bias results.

Key understanding points:

Matching requires a notion of similarity.
Better similarity usually reduces bias, but often at the cost of dropping observations or relying on fewer controls.
Calipers or match restrictions are really design decisions about how much mismatch is tolerable.

What to remember:

Matching is always a compromise between comparability and information retention.
Even in the simplest case, there is no free lunch: strict matching improves comparability but may leave too little data.

Useful caution:

The single-variable example is pedagogically clean, but real applications are harder because relevant confounders are rarely one-dimensional.



4.4 14.4 Multiple Matching Variables
Main idea:

Once several relevant covariates matter at once, matching becomes a multivariate design problem. The chapter moves from intuition to practical methods that define multivariate similarity in different ways.

Why this matters:

This is the section where matching becomes a serious applied tool rather than just a toy example.
It also shows why method choice matters: different matching schemes solve different parts of the multivariate problem and imply different tradeoffs.

Key methods highlighted in the chapter:

Mahalanobis-distance matching:

Uses a distance metric based on the joint covariance structure of the covariates.
Useful when “closeness” should reflect scale and correlation across variables.
Important limitation: close distance does not guarantee strong balance on every variable.

Coarsened exact matching:

Coarsens variables into bins, then performs exact matching on the coarsened version.
Attractive because it is interpretable and lets the analyst control what counts as meaningfully similar.
Important limitation: results depend on coarsening choices, and aggressive coarsening or strict variable sets can drop many units.

Entropy balancing:

Chooses weights to force exact balance on selected covariate moments.
Attractive because the balance target is explicit rather than left to emerge from a search algorithm.
Important limitation: exact balance can be achieved by concentrating weight on a small number of control units.


Worked-example note:

The chapter uses causaldata::black_politicians, which is already familiar from this repo’s lab material.

What to remember:

The main question is not “which algorithm is best in general?”
The real question is “which method gives an acceptable balance-retention-overlap tradeoff for this design?”

Useful caution:

Multivariate matching creates more opportunities for hidden design drift. If units are heavily pruned or weights become extreme, the estimate may stop representing the population the analyst thought they were studying.



4.5 14.5 Propensity Score Weighting with Multiple Matching Variables
Main idea:

The chapter then introduces propensity-score weighting as another way to handle many covariates at once. Instead of matching directly on the full covariate vector, the analyst models treatment assignment and uses the resulting score to construct weights.

Why this matters:

This section helps connect matching logic to a broader balancing framework.
It also shows why the propensity score is appealing: it compresses many covariates into a single object that can guide weighting or adjustment.

Key understanding points:

The propensity score is not the causal estimand; it is a design device.
The score is useful only to the extent that it succeeds in creating acceptable covariate balance.
Modeling treatment assignment does not remove the need for diagnostics.

What to remember:

The chapter’s use of propensity scores is practical rather than reverential.
The point is not that propensity scores are magic; the point is that they are one way of building a better comparison.

Useful caution:

Propensity-score methods are easy to treat as a black box. That is dangerous because a well-fitting treatment model can still produce poor balance, poor overlap, or unstable weights.



4.6 14.6 Assumptions for Matching
Main idea:

This is the chapter’s most important corrective section. After building intuition for matching methods, it states clearly that matching still relies on strong assumptions.

Why this matters:

Readers often absorb the mechanics of matching more quickly than its assumptions.
This section pushes back against the common misunderstanding that a matched sample is automatically a credible causal design.

Key understanding points:

Matching changes the design, but not the fundamental identification logic.
A good match on observables is not the same thing as random assignment.
The chapter’s assumptions section should be read as a warning label for everything before it.


4.6.1 14.6.1 Conditional Independence Assumption
Main idea:

Conditional independence means that once the observed covariates are controlled for, treatment assignment is as good as independent of the potential outcomes.

Why this matters:

This is the core identification assumption behind most observational matching strategies.
Without it, matching only makes the treated and control groups look similar on the variables you measured, not necessarily on the variables that actually drive selection.

What to remember:

This assumption is fundamentally about omitted confounding.
It cannot be verified just by looking at the matched sample.
Rich covariates help, but they do not prove the assumption.

Useful caution:

Matching can make an analysis look disciplined while still failing on the most important unobserved variables.



4.6.2 14.6.2 Common Support
Main idea:

Common support means there must be overlap between treated and control units on the relevant covariate patterns or balancing score.

Why this matters:

If no comparable controls exist for some treated units, the causal question for those units is not answerable with the available data.
In practice, poor support often shows up as discarded observations, poor matches, or extreme weights.

What to remember:

Lack of overlap is not a technical inconvenience; it is a design failure relative to the target comparison.
Trimming to improve overlap can be sensible, but it changes the population being studied.

Useful caution:

Analysts sometimes talk about trimming as if it only improves quality. It also narrows the estimand unless that shift is made explicit.



4.6.3 14.6.3 Balance
Main idea:

Balance is the empirical check that treated and control groups look similar on observed covariates after adjustment.

Why this matters:

Balance is the main observable standard by which a matching or weighting design is judged.
Without good balance, the adjustment has not done the job it was meant to do.

What to remember:

Balance is necessary, not sufficient.
Better balance supports the design, but it does not prove causal identification.
A balance table should be read alongside overlap, retention, and weight dispersion.

Useful caution:

It is easy to focus on a single threshold like absolute standardized mean differences below 0.1 and treat that as the whole story. The deeper question is whether the adjusted comparison is substantively credible.




4.7 14.7 Estimation with Matched Data
Main idea:

After the design has been adjusted and checked, the chapter turns to estimation. This ordering matters: estimation should come after acceptable design work, not before.

Why this matters:

The section reinforces the principle that matching is preprocessing for causal estimation.
It also helps distinguish between the role of weights and the role of the outcome model.

Key understanding points:

The simplest post-matching estimate is often a weighted difference in means.
Regression can still be used after matching or weighting to improve precision or account for remaining structure.
Post-design modeling should not be used to excuse poor design-stage adjustment.


4.7.1 14.7.1 Estimating Mean Differences
Main idea:

Once weights or matched samples are in place, the treatment effect can often be estimated with a simple comparison of adjusted group means.

Why this matters:

This keeps the causal interpretation tied closely to the design.
It also shows that complicated outcome models are not always required if the design has done enough of the comparability work.

What to remember:

Simple estimators are attractive when the design is strong.
But inference is still not trivial because the weights or matching structure were estimated rather than fixed in advance.

Useful caution:

A simple mean difference after a weak design is still weak. Simplicity in estimation is not a substitute for credibility in design.



4.7.2 14.7.2 Combining Matching and Regression
Main idea:

The chapter explicitly allows matching and regression to work together. Matching can improve design, and regression can then model the outcome within the adjusted sample.

Why this matters:

This is often how applied work is actually done.
It avoids the false dichotomy that one must choose either matching or regression.

What to remember:

Matching can reduce model dependence by making treated and control units more comparable before regression.
Regression after matching is most credible when it is modest cleanup after a reasonable design, not a rescue mission for bad overlap or poor balance.

Useful caution:

If the estimate changes dramatically with small changes to the post-match model, that is a warning sign that the design is doing too little on its own.




4.8 14.8 Matching and Treatment Effects
Main idea:

Matching is not just about reducing bias. It also shapes which treatment effect is being estimated.

Why this matters:

This is the link between design mechanics and substantive interpretation.
Two analyses can both be “well matched” and still answer different causal questions if they target different populations.

Key understanding points:

Many matching workflows naturally target the ATT because they prioritize finding controls for treated units.
Other weighting or matching choices can be structured toward the ATE or other estimands.
Sample pruning and weighting concentration can shift the effective target population even when the nominal estimand label stays the same.

What to remember:

Always ask: “effect for whom?”
Estimand choice should guide the design, not be inferred after the algorithm runs.


4.8.1 14.8.1 Getting Other Treatment Effects from Matching
Main idea:

The chapter closes by noting that matching can be adapted to different treatment-effect targets, not just the ATT.

Why this matters:

This is a reminder that weighting and matching procedures are not neutral preprocessing steps. They encode decisions about the comparison population.

What to remember:

Treatment-effect language should be explicit from the start.
If a design keeps all treated units but heavily reweights controls, it is often telling an ATT story.
If the design instead tries to build a population-representative pseudo-sample, it is closer to an ATE story.

Useful caution:

In practice, the declared estimand and the effective estimand can diverge. Trimming, failed overlap, and extreme weights are common reasons.

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
5 Implications for This Repo

The strongest overlap with current repo material is still the black_politicians example, especially for CEM and entropy balancing.
The chapter supports the repo’s existing design-first emphasis, especially the idea that balance, overlap, and retained sample size should be evaluated together.
One gap in the repo relative to the chapter is that propensity-score weighting is not yet given its own compact explainer.
Another useful extension would be a short note on how the chapter’s conceptual language maps onto MatchIt, WeightIt, and cobalt diagnostics in practice.

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
7 Follow-Up Build Plan

Add a short companion section linking each chapter block to relevant repo files or outputs.
Add a compact glossary for terms the chapter uses repeatedly, especially common support, balance, and estimand.
Optionally add a “chapter vs repo emphasis” section showing where this repo goes further on diagnostics, retention, and auditability.
If needed, add a separate replication note for the chapter’s black_politicians workflow using the strict book-default specification.

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
6 High-Level Takeaways

Matching is best thought of as a way to improve comparability, not a guarantee of causal validity.
Weighting is a useful unifying language for understanding what many matching procedures actually do.
Multivariate matching methods differ mainly in how they define acceptable similarity and how they trade off balance against information retention.
The assumptions section is the intellectual center of the chapter: conditional independence, overlap, and balance are the real constraints on credibility.
Estimation after matching should be simple when the design is good and treated with caution when the design is fragile.
Matching methods always imply a target population, whether the analyst states that clearly or not.

title: Notes on The Effect: Matching
text:
Footnotes


A back-door path is a non-causal route of association between treatment and outcome that runs through one or more common causes. In practice, it is the pathway that creates confounding: treatment and outcome move together not because treatment caused the outcome, but because both are related to prior factors.↩︎

title: Notes on The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
text:
3 Executive Summary

The chapter starts with subclassification because it is the clearest way to see the logic of conditioning on observed covariates.
It then shows why exact stratification becomes difficult in realistic settings and uses that difficulty to motivate exact matching and then approximate matching methods.
A recurring theme is that all of these methods are trying to recover credible treated-control comparisons under selection on observables, but they differ in how they define similarity and how much information they discard or reweight.
The chapter is especially relevant to this repo because it connects directly to the repo’s teaching emphasis on design before estimation, on the curse of dimensionality, and on balance-versus-retention tradeoffs.

title: Notes on The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
text:
4 Chapter Structure and Notes

4.1 5.1 Subclassification
Main idea:

Subclassification is introduced as the most straightforward way to make treated and control units comparable: divide the sample into groups defined by covariates, compare treated and untreated outcomes within those groups, and then aggregate those within-group comparisons.

Why this matters:

This gives the reader the cleanest possible version of the matching idea before algorithms get involved.
It makes clear that causal adjustment is fundamentally about comparing like with like.
It also provides the bridge from the backdoor criterion to actual empirical practice.

Key understanding points:

The treatment effect is built from within-stratum comparisons rather than a single raw comparison across the whole sample.
The final estimate is a weighted average of stratum-level effects.
Subclassification works best when the relevant confounders are discrete or can be made meaningfully discrete.

What to remember:

This section is conceptually basic but methodologically important. If subclassification makes sense, the logic of later matching methods makes sense too.
Matching and weighting methods can often be read as more elaborate ways of doing what subclassification tries to do simply.


4.1.1 5.1.1 Some background
Main idea:

The background discussion motivates subclassification by showing how crude mean comparisons can mix treated and untreated units that are not comparable because they differ systematically on pre-treatment variables.

Why this matters:

This is where the chapter makes the inferential problem concrete: observed outcome differences may reflect selection into treatment rather than treatment itself.
Stratifying on a confounder is introduced as a direct fix for that comparability problem.

What to remember:

The point of stratification is not elegance. It is to remove misleading comparisons that pool unlike units.
This section helps anchor the whole chapter in confounding rather than in software procedure.



4.1.2 5.1.2 Identifying assumptions
Main idea:

The chapter ties subclassification to the identifying assumptions needed for observational causal inference, especially the idea that conditioning on the right observed covariates can block confounding pathways.

Why this matters:

This section is the reminder that subclassification is not mechanically causal. It is causal only if the relevant identifying assumptions hold.
It frames the whole chapter as an exercise in achieving conditional comparability, not magical de-biasing.

Key understanding points:

The central assumption is selection on observables or conditional independence after conditioning.
Exchangeability is being claimed within strata, not necessarily in the raw sample.
If important confounders are omitted, subclassification will not solve the problem.

What to remember:

This section is the conceptual foundation for everything that follows.
The later matching methods are all ways of making this identifying logic more workable in high-dimensional settings.



4.1.3 5.1.3 Subclassification exercise: Titanic data set
Main idea:

The Titanic example is used as a simple teaching case to show how subclassification works in practice with discrete support cells.

Why this matters:

It gives the reader a hands-on version of the method before the chapter moves into more technical matching procedures.
It also shows that subclassification is not just theory; it can be implemented transparently with a familiar dataset.

Key understanding points:

The example helps visualize support cells and weighted averaging across strata.
It shows how treatment comparisons can change materially once comparisons are made within more relevant groups.
It is a pedagogic stepping stone to exact matching because the logic is almost identical once strata become exact covariate profiles.

What to remember:

This is a good section to revisit when the later methods start to feel too abstract.
The repo’s titanic appendix is a natural companion to this part of the chapter.



4.1.4 5.1.4 Curse of dimensionality
Main idea:

The curse of dimensionality explains why simple subclassification becomes hard to use once many covariates matter at once: the sample gets split into too many cells, many cells become sparse or empty, and credible within-cell comparisons disappear.

Why this matters:

This is the pivot point of the chapter.
It explains why the simplest design logic cannot just be scaled up naïvely.
It motivates the move from exact stratification to exact matching and then to approximate methods.

Key understanding points:

Every additional covariate or finer binning choice can multiply the number of required strata.
A design can become unusable not because the logic is wrong, but because the data do not support the required fine-grained comparisons.
Approximate matching methods are responses to this sparsity problem.

What to remember:

The curse of dimensionality is not just a computational issue. It is a substantive overlap issue.
Whenever later methods relax exact comparability, this is the reason they do so.




4.2 5.2 Exact Matching
Main idea:

Exact matching takes the subclassification logic to its strict conclusion: treated and control units are compared only when they are identical on the chosen matching covariates.

Why this matters:

Exact matching is conceptually clean and easy to explain.
It makes the design rule explicit and minimizes ambiguity about what counts as a valid comparison.

Key understanding points:

Exact matching is strongest when the covariates are discrete, low-dimensional, or already naturally grouped.
Its main virtue is transparency: the analyst can say exactly which comparisons are allowed.
Its main weakness is feasibility: once the covariate set expands, many treated units may have no exact control counterpart.

What to remember:

Exact matching is the benchmark for interpretability even when it is not feasible.
If exact matching fails badly, that often reveals something important about overlap in the data rather than a mere inconvenience.

Useful caution:

Severe sample loss is not a side issue. It changes the target population and may turn the analysis into a very local statement about a subset with support.



4.3 5.3 Approximate Matching
Main idea:

Approximate matching methods relax the exact-comparison rule so the analyst can still construct usable treated-control comparisons when exact matching is too sparse.

Why this matters:

This is where matching becomes practically useful in real observational data.
The section clarifies that approximation is not abandoning the design goal; it is trying to preserve as much comparability as the data allow.

Key understanding points:

Different approximate methods relax the exactness requirement in different ways.
The central tradeoff is always between closeness of match, retained sample size, and the stability of the resulting design.
Approximate matching therefore requires more diagnostics and more analyst judgment than exact matching.

What to remember:

Approximation is necessary because the data are limited, not because causal standards are lower.
Every approximate method should be judged by the design it produces, not by the elegance of the algorithm.


4.3.1 5.3.1 Nearest neighbor covariate matching
Main idea:

Nearest-neighbor covariate matching defines similarity using a distance metric on the covariates and pairs treated units with the closest available controls.

Why this matters:

This is a natural generalization of exact matching when exact equality is impossible.
It gives a practical rule for choosing among imperfect comparison units.

Key understanding points:

The method depends heavily on how distance is measured.
“Nearest” is a relative statement, not a guarantee of good balance.
Match quality can vary a lot across treated units, especially where overlap is weak.

What to remember:

Distance-based matching is intuitive, but it can hide poor design quality if the nearest available match is still very far away.
Closeness has to be judged substantively and diagnostically, not assumed.



4.3.2 5.3.2 Bias correction
Main idea:

Bias correction is introduced because nearest-neighbor matches are rarely perfect. Even after matching, treated and control units may differ on important covariates.

Why this matters:

This section acknowledges that approximate matching can leave residual imbalance.
It shows one way analysts try to reduce the remaining bias without abandoning the matched design.

Key understanding points:

Bias correction is a post-match adjustment layered on top of the design.
It does not make poor matches good; it only tries to adjust for the remaining mismatch.
The need for bias correction is itself evidence that approximate matching alone may not fully solve the problem.

What to remember:

Bias correction can help, but it should not be confused with a cure for bad overlap.
The more correction the design seems to need, the weaker the underlying matched comparison may be.



4.3.3 5.3.3 Propensity score methods
Main idea:

Propensity score methods are presented as a way to reduce the dimensionality problem by summarizing many covariates into a single balancing score: the probability of treatment given the observed covariates.

Why this matters:

This is one of the chapter’s most important responses to the curse of dimensionality.
It explains why analysts often use propensity scores when direct matching on the full covariate vector is impractical.

Key understanding points:

The attraction of the propensity score is dimensional reduction, not guaranteed success.
Units with similar propensity scores are meant to be similar in their observed treatment-assignment profile.
The propensity score is useful only if it actually produces acceptable covariate balance after implementation.

What to remember:

The propensity score is a design device, not the estimand and not the final causal argument.
Good propensity score practice still requires overlap checks and balance diagnostics.

Useful caution:

It is easy to mistake a well-specified treatment model for a good design. The chapter’s structure implies these are not the same thing.



4.3.4 5.3.4 Example: The NSW job training program
Main idea:

The NSW job training example is used as a benchmark setting for assessing how well observational adjustment methods can recover effects relative to a more credible experimental reference.

Why this matters:

This example is central to the matching literature because it provides a way to judge whether a matching or weighting approach is actually getting close to a known benchmark.
It moves the discussion from abstract method comparison to empirical performance.

Key understanding points:

The example is not only about one program; it is about whether observational adjustment can approximate an experimental benchmark.
It highlights how design choices can materially affect the estimated effect.
It also reinforces the need to assess methods comparatively rather than doctrinally.

What to remember:

This is one of the strongest chapter links to current repo material because the repo already uses the NSW/CPS benchmark logic.
The value of the example is that it disciplines method evaluation with an external reference point.



4.3.5 5.3.5 Weighting on the propensity score
Main idea:

Propensity scores can be used not only for matching but also for weighting, so the adjusted sample behaves like a pseudo-population in which treatment is less confounded by observed covariates.

Why this matters:

This widens the chapter from pairwise or stratum-based comparisons into a broader weighting framework.
It also makes the link to estimands more explicit because different weights correspond to different target populations.

Key understanding points:

Weighting changes the influence of observations rather than necessarily dropping them.
Weight instability becomes a central issue when estimated treatment probabilities are near zero or one.
Overlap problems show up here as extreme weights rather than only as failed matches.

What to remember:

Weighting can preserve more units than strict matching, but it can still create fragile designs if a few observations carry too much influence.
Effective sample size matters as much as nominal sample size.



4.3.6 5.3.6 Nearest-neighbor matching
Main idea:

The chapter then treats nearest-neighbor matching in its more operational form, where design decisions such as replacement, order, and implementation details affect the resulting matched sample.

Why this matters:

This is where a seemingly simple idea becomes a real design workflow with choices that affect both bias and variance.
It reminds the reader that matching methods are not single fixed procedures.

Key understanding points:

Matching with replacement can improve match quality but may concentrate influence on a small number of controls.
Matching without replacement may spread influence more evenly but can force worse matches later in the procedure.
Inference depends on the structure created by the design, not only on the eventual regression or mean difference formula.

What to remember:

Operational choices in nearest-neighbor matching are not minor implementation details. They define the actual design.
Good matching is judged by the resulting balance and overlap, not by the label “nearest neighbor” alone.



4.3.7 5.3.7 Coarsened exact matching
Main idea:

Coarsened exact matching is presented as a compromise: exact matching is performed, but on coarsened versions of the covariates rather than on the raw covariates themselves.

Why this matters:

This gives the analyst direct control over what counts as meaningfully similar while avoiding the full sparsity problem of raw exact matching.
It is one of the clearest design-oriented methods in the chapter because its rules are explicit and interpretable.

Key understanding points:

Coarsening is not a technical nuisance; it is the main design choice.
Different cutpoints can materially change both balance and sample retention.
CEM often makes the balance-retention tradeoff more visible than black-box distance methods.

What to remember:

CEM is attractive because it keeps the matching rule understandable.
But it still requires iterative checking because overly fine coarsening can drop too much sample, while overly coarse binning can weaken comparability.




4.4 5.4 Conclusion
Main idea:

The conclusion appears to pull together subclassification, exact matching, and approximate matching as related attempts to recover credible counterfactual comparisons from observational data.

Why this matters:

The chapter’s ending is important because it clarifies what the methods are for and, equally, what they cannot do.

What to remember:

The core lesson is not that one matching method wins universally.
The deeper lesson is that observational adjustment is a design problem shaped by overlap, dimensionality, and identifying assumptions.
Matching methods are useful when they make comparisons more credible and more transparent, not when they hide weak support behind technical language.

title: Notes on The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
text:
5 Implications for This Repo

The chapter overlaps strongly with the repo’s existing main report, the titanic teaching appendix, and the NSW/CPS benchmark lab.
The subclassification and curse-of-dimensionality sections are especially useful because they explain why the repo emphasizes design tradeoffs rather than presenting methods as plug-and-play tools.
The NSW example provides the clearest direct bridge to current benchmark materials already in the repo.
A gap relative to the chapter is that the repo does not yet have a compact note page devoted specifically to subclassification as the conceptual precursor to matching.

title: Notes on The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
text:
7 Follow-Up Build Plan

Add direct links from subclassification, exact matching, and NSW sections to the relevant repo reports and labs.
Add a small glossary for recurring terms such as subclassification, balancing score, and common support.
Optionally add a short “Mixtape versus current repo emphasis” section, especially on diagnostics and retained sample size.
If needed, add a separate note that isolates the NSW benchmark workflow from the rest of the chapter.

title: Notes on The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
text:
6 High-Level Takeaways

Subclassification is the cleanest entry point for understanding matching because it makes the conditioning logic visible.
The curse of dimensionality is the main reason exact stratification breaks down in realistic settings.
Exact matching is transparent but often infeasible once the design involves multiple meaningful covariates.
Approximate matching methods differ mainly in how they relax exactness and how they manage the balance-retention tradeoff.
Propensity score methods solve a dimensionality problem only if they actually improve balance and maintain overlap.
Benchmark examples such as NSW matter because they let analysts evaluate designs against a stronger reference than intuition alone.
