title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
3 Executive Summary

Matching = build an artificial comparison group from untreated units that resemble treated units on observed baseline characteristics.
Exact matching is conceptually clean but quickly runs into the curse of dimensionality.
Propensity-score matching is the chapter’s main practical workaround.
The non-negotiables are baseline covariates, common support, and the absence of important unobserved selection.
The HISP example shows that richer baseline covariates produce more credible matches.
The chapter’s strongest practical advice is to combine matching with difference-in-differences when baseline outcomes exist.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
4 Chapter Structure and Notes

4.1 Chapter 8. Matching
Main idea:

The chapter frames matching as a way to build an artificial comparison group from untreated units that resemble treated units on observed baseline characteristics.

Why this matters:

This is one of the core practical responses to non-random assignment in programme evaluation.
It offers a design-based alternative to simply comparing all participants to all nonparticipants.

Key understanding points:

Matching is about comparability on observables measured before treatment.
The purpose is to approximate the counterfactual outcome for treated units by using untreated units that look similar at baseline.
The chapter sits naturally after earlier material on randomized assignment and difference-in-differences because it addresses a setting where the rule of assignment is less clean.

What to remember:

Matching is only as credible as the baseline variables used to construct the comparison.
The artificial comparison group is “artificial” because it is selected by design rather than given by the original assignment process.



4.2 8.1 Exact Matching on Four Characteristics
Main idea:

The chapter first introduces exact matching in a simple form: treated and untreated units are matched only when they are identical on a small set of selected baseline characteristics.

Why this matters:

This is the most transparent version of matching.
It shows clearly what the analyst is trying to do before the chapter moves to more flexible methods.

Key understanding points:

Exact matching works best when the covariates are discrete or can be sensibly grouped.
The main advantage is interpretability: everyone can see what counts as a valid comparison.
The main limitation is that many units may remain unmatched once several characteristics are required simultaneously.

What to remember:

Exact matching is the clearest conceptual benchmark even when it becomes impractical.
If exact matching fails because few or no matches exist, that is evidence of limited overlap rather than a mere technical inconvenience.

Useful caution:

Sample loss under exact matching can alter the target population. The resulting effect may apply only to the subset for whom exact counterparts exist.



4.3 8.2 Propensity Score Matching and Common Support
Main idea:

The chapter then introduces propensity score matching as a way to make matching feasible when exact matching on many observed characteristics is too sparse.

Why this matters:

The propensity score reduces a multivariate baseline profile to a single summary of treatment likelihood based on observed characteristics.
This makes it easier to search for comparable untreated units when the covariate space is large.

Key understanding points:

The chapter ties propensity score matching directly to common support, meaning the range of scores where treated and untreated units overlap sufficiently to be compared.
Without common support, a propensity-score match can still be forced mathematically, but it will not be substantively credible.
The score itself is not the goal; the goal is better covariate comparability.

What to remember:

Propensity score matching is a solution to a dimensionality problem, not a guarantee of valid causal inference.
Common-support checking is central, not optional.

Useful caution:

Units outside common support are telling you that the available untreated sample does not provide a plausible counterfactual for some treated cases.



4.4 8.3 Matching for HISP: Common Support
Main idea:

The chapter applies the common-support logic to the HISP example, using a concrete programme evaluation setting rather than only an abstract matching discussion.

Why this matters:

This is where the method becomes operational.
It shows that support problems are not abstract textbook caveats; they determine which units can actually contribute to the estimated effect.

Key understanding points:

Some treated and untreated units may not have comparable counterparts once baseline covariates are taken seriously.
Matching therefore may exclude observations or narrow the effective sample to the region where comparison is credible.
The HISP example appears to be used as a running case so the reader can compare how estimates change across methods.

What to remember:

Common support determines the population for which the method can learn anything credible.
If support is weak, the honest conclusion may be that the effect is only identified for a narrower subset.



4.5 Propensity Score Matching
Main idea:

This section likely pulls the previous intuition together into a more operational description of propensity score matching as a design workflow.

Why this matters:

It reminds the reader that matching is not just a concept. It requires estimation, construction of matches, and diagnostic checking.

Key understanding points:

The workflow probably includes:

estimating treatment probabilities from baseline covariates
matching treated and untreated units on the estimated score
dropping units outside support if needed
checking whether balance improves after matching

The purpose of the score is to help build comparable groups, not to replace later diagnostic judgment.

What to remember:

Propensity score matching should be judged by the quality of the resulting matched sample, not by the sophistication of the score model.



4.6 8.1 Estimating the Propensity Score Based on Baseline Observed Characteristics
Main idea:

The chapter explicitly separates the estimation of the propensity score from the later effect-estimation steps.

Why this matters:

This keeps the analyst focused on design first.
It also makes clear that the score must be based on baseline observed characteristics only.

Key understanding points:

Matching covariates should be measured before the programme could affect them.
Including post-treatment variables would contaminate the design because those variables may already contain treatment effects.
The estimated propensity score is a summary of baseline selection patterns, not an outcome model.

What to remember:

Baseline really means pre-treatment.
Propensity score estimation is part of building the comparison group, not part of proving causal validity.



4.7 8.2 Evaluating HISP: Matching on Baseline Characteristics and Comparison of Means
Main idea:

After matching is built, the chapter appears to estimate the programme effect in the HISP example using a simple comparison of means across the matched groups.

Why this matters:

This shows the cleanest version of post-match estimation.
It lets the reader see the effect of design adjustment without immediately layering on regression.

Key understanding points:

The comparison of means after matching answers a different question than the raw difference in means from the unmatched sample.
The matched estimate is meant to reflect comparisons among more similar baseline units.
This keeps the effect estimate closely tied to the design logic of the chapter.

What to remember:

A simple estimator is most interpretable when the design is strong.
If the matched comparison still looks fragile, a simple mean difference will not rescue it.



4.8 8.3 Evaluating HISP: Matching on Baseline Characteristics and Regression Analysis
Main idea:

The chapter then combines matching with regression analysis in the HISP application.

Why this matters:

This reflects common applied practice: matching improves comparability first, and regression is then used within the matched sample to improve precision or account for remaining baseline differences.

Key understanding points:

Matching and regression are complements here, not substitutes.
The regression is most credible when it comes after a reasonably balanced matched design.
If the regression is doing too much corrective work after poor matching, the analyst may be relying on modeling rather than on design.

What to remember:

Post-match regression is strongest as a modest refinement, not as a rescue strategy.
Large shifts between the simple matched mean comparison and the matched regression estimate may be a sign that the underlying design is weak.



4.9 8.4 Evaluating HISP: Difference-in-Differences Combined with Matching on Baseline Characteristics
Main idea:

The chapter then shows matching combined with difference-in-differences in the HISP case.

Why this matters:

This is probably one of the chapter’s most practically important moves.
It shows that matching can be used to improve baseline comparability before applying a time-based identification strategy.

Key understanding points:

Difference-in-differences addresses time-invariant differences under a parallel-trends logic.
Matching, in this combined setup, likely helps make the treatment and comparison groups more alike at baseline before the before-after contrast is taken.
This combination can be more credible than matching alone when panel or repeated-outcome information is available.

What to remember:

Matching does not have to compete with stronger quasi-experimental designs. It can sometimes support them.
The key question is what each component contributes: matching improves baseline comparability, while DiD leverages outcome changes over time.



4.10 Combining Matching with Other Methods
Main idea:

The chapter explicitly frames matching as compatible with other impact-evaluation methods.

Why this matters:

This avoids a false choice between “matching” and “regression” or between “matching” and “difference-in-differences.”
It positions matching as one component of an evaluator workflow rather than a self-contained ideology.

Key understanding points:

Combining methods can strengthen credibility when each method addresses a distinct design problem.
Combining methods can also become superficial if matching quality is poor and later modeling is simply covering that weakness.

What to remember:

The right sequence is design first, then estimation.
Adding more methods is not automatically better; it is only better when each added layer solves a real problem.



4.11 Limitations of the Matching Method
Main idea:

The chapter includes a dedicated limitations section to make clear that matching is not a cure-all.

Why this matters:

This is the main safeguard against overinterpreting a matched estimate.
It reminds the reader that better observed comparability is not the same as causal certainty.

Likely limitations emphasized by the chapter structure:

Matching only addresses differences in observed baseline characteristics.
Unobserved confounders can still bias the estimate.
Weak common support can force the analyst to drop many units or rely on poor matches.
Results may depend on which covariates are chosen and how the matching is implemented.

What to remember:

Matching is a design improvement, not a guarantee.
Sample loss, overlap problems, and sensitivity to matching rules all affect the meaning of the final estimate.



4.12 Checklist: Matching
Main idea:

The chapter closes with a practical checklist, which fits the book’s implementation-oriented style.

Why this matters:

The checklist is likely the most directly reusable part for teaching or applied evaluation work.

Probable checklist items based on the chapter flow:

identify the treatment and comparison groups clearly
choose covariates that are measured at baseline and plausibly related to selection and outcomes
use exact matching where feasible and propensity score matching where dimensionality makes exact matching impractical
inspect common support before trusting the matched design
check whether matching actually improved covariate balance
estimate treatment effects only after the design looks acceptable
be explicit about what subset of units remains in the matched sample
consider whether regression or difference-in-differences should be layered on after matching

What to remember:

The checklist reinforces that matching is a workflow, not a single line of code.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
9 Implications for This Repo

Matching should be framed here as a design-repair tool under selection on observables, not as a generic causal estimator.
Any matching workflow should make baseline measurement rules explicit and refuse post-treatment covariates by default.
Diagnostics should be treated as core outputs, especially overlap and balance checks, not as optional appendix material.
When baseline outcomes are available, the repo should prefer matched DiD over matching alone.
Cases with a single treated unit or a small number of aggregate units are better mapped to synthetic-control logic than to household-level nearest-neighbor matching.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
7 Follow-Up Build Plan

Clarify the programme details and acronym expansion for HISP once better text extraction or manual reading is available.
Add short glossary footnotes for terms such as common support, propensity score, and artificial comparison group.
Add direct repo cross-links where the chapter aligns with current exact-matching, weighting, and benchmark materials.
Consider a future teaching note comparing Gertler’s evaluator workflow with the more methods-centric framing in The Effect and The Mixtape.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
10 High-Level Takeaways

Matching can improve comparability, but it does not eliminate selection bias by itself.
The credibility of the design depends on baseline covariates, overlap, and knowledge of the enrollment process.
The chapter consistently points toward combination designs, especially matched DiD, when the data allow it.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
4 Study Notes

4.1 Constructing an Artificial Comparison Group
Main idea:

Matching is for settings where treatment assignment was not randomized and there is no clean eligibility rule to lean on.
The evaluator tries to find untreated units that look as similar as possible to treated units before treatment.

Why this matters:

The whole method is trying to approximate the counterfactual using design choices inside the data.
The target is not “similarity” in a vague sense. The target is similarity on characteristics that help explain enrollment.

What to remember:

Matching is a selection-process method.
The quality of the comparison group depends on whether the data capture the variables that drove take-up.



4.2 Key Concept: Observed Characteristics Only
Main idea:

The chapter’s short definition is that matching uses large data sets and statistical techniques to construct the best possible comparison group based on observed characteristics.

Implications:

Matching can only adjust for what is measured.
Bigger, richer baseline data sets help.
Unobserved confounding remains the core vulnerability.

Watch for:

The chapter’s exact-matching example is there to show why this gets hard fast once many relevant covariates matter at the same time.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
5 Propensity-Score Matching

5.1 Why the Chapter Moves Beyond Exact Matching
Main idea:

Propensity-score matching is introduced as the fix for the curse of dimensionality.
Instead of exact agreement on every covariate, units are matched on estimated enrollment probabilities.

What to remember:

The propensity score is a design tool, not the estimand.
The point is to produce a matched sample with better covariate comparability.



5.2 Baseline Covariates Are Non-Negotiable
Main idea:

Only pre-treatment observed characteristics should go into the propensity score.

Why:

Post-treatment variables may already contain treatment effects.
Matching on them can make treated and control units look artificially similar for the wrong reason.

What to remember:

Pre-treatment measurement is part of the identification logic, not a nice-to-have.



5.3 Common Support
Main idea:

Matching only works where treated and untreated units overlap in propensity-score space.

Why this matters:

No overlap means no credible counterfactual for some treated units.
In that case the estimate only applies to the subset on common support.

Watch for:

weak overlap at the tails
forced matches that look mathematical but not substantive
sample restriction changing the target population

Evaluator workflow in the chapter:

Obtain representative, comparable data on enrolled and nonenrolled units.
Pool the samples and estimate enrollment probabilities from observed characteristics.
Restrict to the region of common support.
Match each enrolled unit to similar nonenrolled units.
Compare outcomes for treated units and their matched comparisons.
Average those unit-level impacts to obtain an overall effect estimate.



5.4 What Matching Can and Cannot Do
Recurring cautions:

matching only accounts for observed characteristics
matching should not rely on post-treatment covariates
the choice of matching variables matters most when those variables actually determine enrollment

Bottom line:

Good software cannot rescue a bad covariate set or weak knowledge of the assignment process.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
6 Combining Matching with Other Methods

6.1 Matched Difference-in-Differences
Main idea:

If baseline outcomes exist, the chapter prefers matching plus DiD over matching alone.

Why this matters:

Matching handles comparability on observables.
DiD helps remove bias from unobserved differences that are fixed over time.

Workflow:

Match on observed baseline characteristics.
Compute before-after outcome changes for treated units.
Compute before-after outcome changes for their matched comparisons.
Subtract the two changes.
Average the resulting double differences.

What to remember:

Matching alone is relatively weak.
Matching plus DiD is stronger because it adds a second identification layer.



6.2 Synthetic Control
Main idea:

Synthetic control is the single-treated-unit extension of the chapter’s matching logic.

What it does:

It builds a weighted synthetic comparison unit rather than choosing one untreated match.

What to remember:

It needs a long pre-treatment time series.
The Basque Country example shows why naive comparisons can fail when pre-treatment determinants already differ.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
7 HISP Worked Example

7.1 Enrollment Model
Main idea:

HISP is the chapter’s worked example for how matching is actually implemented.

Baseline predictors used in the richer specification:

age of the household head and spouse
head and spouse education
whether the household head is female
indigenous status
household size
dirt floor
bathroom access
hectares of land
distance to hospital

What the table suggests:

HISP appears to be targeted toward poorer and less-educated households.
Enrollment is more likely for indigenous households, larger households, households with dirt floors, and households farther from hospitals.
Enrollment is less likely for older, more educated households and households with bathrooms or more land.



7.2 Common Support and Match Construction
Main idea:

With the full covariate set, the chapter reports full common support for enrolled households.
The example uses nearest-neighbor matching.

What to remember:

In this worked example, every enrolled household gets a matched nonenrolled household.
That is a property of this sample and specification, not a general guarantee.



7.3 Effect Estimates Reported in the Export
Keep these figures in mind:

Matched comparison of means:

enrolled household health expenditures: 7.84
matched comparison household health expenditures: 17.79
estimated effect: -9.95 US dollars

Matched regression:

full explanatory set: -9.95** with standard error (0.24)
limited explanatory set: -11.35** with standard error (0.22)

Matched DiD:

baseline difference: 0.54
follow-up difference: -9.95
matched DiD estimate: -9.41** with standard error (0.19)


Study note:

The full covariate set is the more credible design in the chapter’s own logic.
The matched DiD result being close to the simple matched estimate is a useful consistency check inside the example.



7.4 Why the Limited Covariate Set Matters
Main idea:

The chapter uses a thin specification to show that “using matching” is not the same as “having a credible design.”

What this example teaches:

it shows why substantive knowledge of enrollment is essential
it warns against treating propensity-score matching as a push-button correction for selection bias



7.5 HISP Study Questions
The chapter ends by pushing the reader to ask:

What assumptions are needed to accept the matching estimates?
Why do results differ between the full and limited explanatory-variable sets?
How do the matching results compare with randomized assignment, and why?
Should HISP be scaled up nationally on this basis?

Why these questions matter:

They force a shift from software output to design credibility.

title: Notes on Gertler et al.: Matching
text:
8 Limitations and Checklist

8.1 Limitations of Matching
Main idea:

The chapter is blunt that matching has serious weaknesses.

Main limits:

it requires large, information-rich data sets
it may fail because of limited common support
it cannot adjust for unobserved determinants of both participation and outcomes
the key identifying assumption about no relevant unobserved differences is strong and untestable

What to remember:

Matching is treated as weaker than randomized assignment, IV, and RDD.
The authors present it as a fallback or complement, not a first-best design.
Ex post matching is especially risky if baseline covariates must be reconstructed after treatment starts.



8.2 Checklist: Matching
Use this as the quick recall test:

Is participation driven by unobserved variables that also affect outcomes?
Are observed baseline characteristics well balanced after matching?
Can a matched comparison unit be found for each treated unit, indicating adequate common support?

Quick interpretation:

If any of these answers look weak, the matching design is weak.

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
Use this page as a guided replication worksheet for the HISP case-study material from Impact Evaluation in Practice.

Run the sections in the same order as the original Stata do-file.
Treat the early methods as design contrasts, not as equally credible estimates.
Keep notes on how the estimate moves as the design gets stronger.
Use the evaluated-code page when you want the processed outputs immediately.







Note



Unlike the other labs in this repository, this page is organized to follow the logic of data/IE in practice files/HISP Book replication.do as closely as possible. The aim is to translate that Stata workflow into R, not to rewrite it into a different teaching sequence.

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
Use this page as a guided replication worksheet for the HISP case-study material from Impact Evaluation in Practice.

Run the sections in the same order as the original Stata do-file.
Treat the early methods as design contrasts, not as equally credible estimates.
Keep notes on how the estimate moves as the design gets stronger.
Use the evaluated-code page when you want the processed outputs immediately.







Note



Unlike the other labs in this repository, this page is organized to follow the logic of data/IE in practice files/HISP Book replication.do as closely as possible. The aim is to translate that Stata workflow into R, not to rewrite it into a different teaching sequence.

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
2 Source Baseline
This lab is based on the HISP case-study files in data/IE in practice files:

primary workflow baseline: HISP Book replication.do
input dataset: evaluation.dta
converted analysis dataset used here: evaluation.csv

The full R workflow also exports the other Stata files in the folder to CSV so the conversion step is explicit and reproducible.

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
section: 3 Step 1: Convert The Stata Files And Load The HISP Data
text:
3 Step 1: Convert The Stata Files And Load The HISP Data

required_packages <- c(
  "haven",
  "readr",
  "dplyr",
  "ggplot2",
  "MatchIt",
  "sandwich",
  "boot",
  "broom",
  "nlme"
)

missing_packages <- required_packages[!vapply(
  required_packages,
  requireNamespace,
  logical(1),
  quietly = TRUE
)]

if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  install.packages(missing_packages, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

data_dir <- "../../data/IE in practice files"
dta_files <- list.files(data_dir, pattern = "[.]dta$", full.names = TRUE)

csv_export_manifest <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(dta_files, function(dta_path) {
  csv_path <- sub("[.]dta$", ".csv", dta_path)
  dat <- haven::read_dta(dta_path)
  readr::write_csv(dat, csv_path)

  data.frame(
    dta_file = basename(dta_path),
    csv_file = basename(csv_path),
    rows = nrow(dat),
    columns = ncol(dat)
  )
}))

dat <- readr::read_csv(file.path(data_dir, "evaluation.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

controls <- c(
  "age_hh",
  "age_sp",
  "educ_hh",
  "educ_sp",
  "female_hh",
  "indigenous",
  "hhsize",
  "dirtfloor",
  "bathroom",
  "land",
  "hospital_distance"
)

dat |>
  dplyr::select(
    household_identifier,
    locality_identifier,
    treatment_locality,
    promotion_locality,
    eligible,
    enrolled,
    round,
    health_expenditures
  ) |>
  head(10)

Checkpoint:

Are the CSV exports now present beside the original .dta files?
Does evaluation.csv keep the same 19,827 rows and 22 columns as the Stata source?

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
section: 4 Step 2: Method 1, Before-After Among Enrolled Households
text:
4 Step 2: Method 1, Before-After Among Enrolled Households
The Stata do-file starts with the simplest comparison: enrolled households in treatment localities, before versus after.

method_1_dat <- dat |>
  dplyr::filter(treatment_locality == 1, enrolled == 1)

t.test(
  health_expenditures ~ factor(round, levels = c(0, 1)),
  data = method_1_dat,
  var.equal = TRUE
)

fit_m1_raw <- lm(health_expenditures ~ round, data = method_1_dat)
sandwich::vcovCL(fit_m1_raw, cluster = method_1_dat$locality_identifier, type = "HC1")

fit_m1_controls <- lm(
  health_expenditures ~ round + age_hh + age_sp + educ_hh + educ_sp +
    female_hh + indigenous + hhsize + dirtfloor + bathroom + land + hospital_distance,
  data = method_1_dat
)

What to note:

This estimate is easy to compute but vulnerable to time trends.
The control-adjusted version follows the do-file, but it does not solve the design problem.

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
section: 5 Step 3: Method 2, Enrolled Versus Not Enrolled At Follow-Up
text:
5 Step 3: Method 2, Enrolled Versus Not Enrolled At Follow-Up
This is the second naive comparison in the book replication script: follow-up outcomes in treatment localities, comparing enrolled and nonenrolled households.

method_2_dat <- dat |>
  dplyr::filter(treatment_locality == 1, round == 1)

t.test(
  health_expenditures ~ factor(enrolled, levels = c(0, 1)),
  data = method_2_dat,
  var.equal = TRUE
)

fit_m2_raw <- lm(health_expenditures ~ enrolled, data = method_2_dat)
fit_m2_controls <- lm(
  health_expenditures ~ enrolled + age_hh + age_sp + educ_hh + educ_sp +
    female_hh + indigenous + hhsize + dirtfloor + bathroom + land + hospital_distance,
  data = method_2_dat
)

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
section: 6 Step 4: Method 3, Randomized Assignment Among Eligible Households

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
section: 7 Step 5: Method 4, Instrumental Variables Under Random Promotion
text:
7 Step 5: Method 4, Instrumental Variables Under Random Promotion
The IV branch uses promotion_locality as the instrument for enrolled_rp at follow-up.

method_4_dat <- dat |>
  dplyr::filter(round == 1)

t.test(
  enrolled_rp ~ factor(promotion_locality, levels = c(0, 1)),
  data = method_4_dat,
  var.equal = TRUE
)

first_stage <- lm(
  enrolled_rp ~ promotion_locality + age_hh + age_sp + educ_hh + educ_sp +
    female_hh + indigenous + hhsize + dirtfloor + bathroom + land + hospital_distance,
  data = method_4_dat
)

# The full script computes the second stage with a cluster-robust 2SLS helper
# so the R output matches the Stata ivreg logic more closely.
source("hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R", local = TRUE)
method_4_iv_regressions

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
8 Step 6: Method 5, Regression Discontinuity
The do-file normalizes the poverty index around the 58 cutoff and estimates a piecewise-linear discontinuity model in treatment localities.

method_5_dat <- dat |>
  dplyr::filter(treatment_locality == 1) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    poverty_index_left = ifelse(poverty_index <= 58, poverty_index - 58, 0),
    poverty_index_right = ifelse(poverty_index > 58, poverty_index - 58, 0)
  )

fit_rdd_followup <- lm(
  health_expenditures ~ poverty_index_left + poverty_index_right + eligible,
  data = dplyr::filter(method_5_dat, round == 1)
)

fit_rdd_controls <- lm(
  health_expenditures ~ eligible + poverty_index_left + poverty_index_right +
    age_hh + age_sp + educ_hh + educ_sp + female_hh + indigenous +
    hhsize + dirtfloor + bathroom + land + hospital_distance,
  data = dplyr::filter(method_5_dat, round == 1)
)

ggplot2::ggplot(
  dplyr::filter(method_5_dat, round == 1, health_expenditures < 60),
  ggplot2::aes(x = poverty_index, y = health_expenditures)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.12) +
  ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = 58, linetype = "dashed")

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
9 Step 7: Method 6, Difference-in-Differences
The DiD branch stays in treatment localities and adds the enrolled * round interaction.

method_6_dat <- dat |>
  dplyr::filter(treatment_locality == 1) |>
  dplyr::mutate(enrolled_round = enrolled * round)

t.test(
  health_expenditures ~ factor(round, levels = c(0, 1)),
  data = dplyr::filter(method_6_dat, enrolled == 0),
  var.equal = TRUE
)

t.test(
  health_expenditures ~ factor(round, levels = c(0, 1)),
  data = dplyr::filter(method_6_dat, enrolled == 1),
  var.equal = TRUE
)

fit_did_raw <- lm(
  health_expenditures ~ enrolled_round + round + enrolled,
  data = method_6_dat
)

fit_did_controls <- lm(
  health_expenditures ~ enrolled_round + round + enrolled +
    age_hh + age_sp + educ_hh + educ_sp + female_hh + indigenous +
    hhsize + dirtfloor + bathroom + land + hospital_distance,
  data = method_6_dat
)

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
10 Step 8: Method 7, Propensity-Score Matching
The matching section in the do-file reshapes the panel to wide form, estimates a probit propensity score, randomizes row order, and runs nearest-neighbor matching first on a restricted covariate set and then on the full control set.

wide_dat <- reshape(
  as.data.frame(dat),
  direction = "wide",
  idvar = "household_identifier",
  timevar = "round",
  sep = ""
)

coalesce_pair <- function(left, right) ifelse(is.na(left), right, left)

for (var_name in c(
  "enrolled", "age_hh", "age_sp", "educ_hh", "educ_sp", "female_hh",
  "indigenous", "hhsize", "dirtfloor", "bathroom", "land", "hospital_distance"
)) {
  wide_dat[[var_name]] <- coalesce_pair(wide_dat[[paste0(var_name, "0")]], wide_dat[[paste0(var_name, "1")]])
}

matching_dat <- wide_dat[
  complete.cases(wide_dat[, c(
    "enrolled",
    "health_expenditures0",
    "health_expenditures1",
    "age_hh",
    "educ_hh",
    "age_sp",
    "educ_sp",
    "female_hh",
    "indigenous",
    "hhsize",
    "dirtfloor",
    "bathroom",
    "land",
    "hospital_distance"
  )]),
]

probit_fit <- glm(
  enrolled ~ age_hh + educ_hh,
  data = matching_dat,
  family = binomial(link = "probit")
)

matching_dat$pscore <- predict(probit_fit, type = "response")

set.seed(100)
m_restricted <- MatchIt::matchit(
  enrolled ~ age_hh + educ_hh,
  data = matching_dat,
  method = "nearest",
  distance = "glm",
  link = "probit",
  ratio = 1,
  replace = FALSE,
  m.order = "random",
  estimand = "ATT",
  normalize = FALSE
)

matched_restricted <- MatchIt::match.data(m_restricted)
lm(health_expenditures1 ~ enrolled, data = matched_restricted, weights = weights)

set.seed(100)
m_full <- MatchIt::matchit(
  enrolled ~ age_hh + age_sp + educ_hh + educ_sp + female_hh + indigenous +
    hhsize + dirtfloor + bathroom + land + hospital_distance,
  data = matching_dat,
  method = "nearest",
  distance = "glm",
  link = "probit",
  ratio = 1,
  replace = FALSE,
  m.order = "random",
  estimand = "ATT",
  normalize = FALSE
)

For the full-set branch, swap age_hh + educ_hh for the full control list used earlier.

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
12 Step 10: Power Calculations
The final branch follows the book’s power-calculation logic using follow-up treated means, standard deviations, and ICC estimates.

two_sample_n_per_arm <- function(sd_outcome, minimum_detectable_effect, power, alpha = 0.05) {
  z_alpha <- qnorm(1 - alpha / 2)
  z_beta <- qnorm(power)
  ceiling(((z_alpha + z_beta)^2 * (sd_outcome^2 + sd_outcome^2)) / minimum_detectable_effect^2)
}

power_dat <- dat |>
  dplyr::filter(eligible == 1, round == 1, treatment_locality == 1)

m1 <- mean(power_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE)
sd1 <- sd(power_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE)

data.frame(
  minimum_detectable_effect = c(1, 2, 3),
  target_mean = m1 - c(1, 2, 3),
  n_per_arm_90 = vapply(c(1, 2, 3), two_sample_n_per_arm, numeric(1), sd_outcome = sd1, power = 0.9),
  n_per_arm_80 = vapply(c(1, 2, 3), two_sample_n_per_arm, numeric(1), sd_outcome = sd1, power = 0.8)
)

title: Lab: HISP IE in Practice
text:
13 Comparison Question
After running the full sequence, compare the main effect terms across methods:

Which branches are obviously biased because the design is too weak?
Which branches move closest to the more credible design-based estimates?
How much do the estimates shift once the workflow reaches RDD, DiD, IV, and matching?

The evaluated-code page collects those outputs into one place, and the QA log documents where the R workflow intentionally mirrors the Stata source.

title: HISP IE in Practice: Full Code
text:
This document contains the complete executable R script for hisp_ie_practice_lab.qmd.
# HISP IE in Practice lab: full R reproduction of the main HISP book replication do-file.
# Run with:
#   Rscript docs/labs/hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R

options(width = 120)

required_packages <- c(
  "haven",
  "readr",
  "dplyr",
  "ggplot2",
  "MatchIt",
  "sandwich",
  "boot",
  "broom",
  "nlme"
)

missing_packages <- required_packages[!vapply(
  required_packages,
  requireNamespace,
  logical(1),
  quietly = TRUE
)]

if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  message("Installing missing packages: ", paste(missing_packages, collapse = ", "))
  install.packages(missing_packages, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

get_script_path <- function() {
  for (idx in rev(seq_along(sys.frames()))) {
    if (!is.null(sys.frame(idx)$ofile)) {
      return(normalizePath(sys.frame(idx)$ofile))
    }
  }

  cmd_args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = FALSE)
  file_arg <- "--file="
  matches <- grep(file_arg, cmd_args)

  if (length(matches) > 0) {
    candidate <- sub(file_arg, "", cmd_args[matches[1]])

    if (grepl("hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code[.]R$", candidate)) {
      return(normalizePath(candidate))
    }
  }

  normalizePath("docs/labs/hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R")
}

ensure_csv_exports <- function(dir_path) {
  dta_files <- list.files(dir_path, pattern = "[.]dta$", full.names = TRUE)

  dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(dta_files, function(dta_path) {
    csv_path <- sub("[.]dta$", ".csv", dta_path)
    dat <- haven::read_dta(dta_path)
    readr::write_csv(dat, csv_path)

    data.frame(
      dta_file = basename(dta_path),
      csv_file = basename(csv_path),
      rows = nrow(dat),
      columns = ncol(dat),
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }))
}

get_named_value <- function(x, key) {
  key <- as.character(key)

  if (!key %in% names(x)) {
    return(NA_real_)
  }

  unname(as.numeric(x[[key]]))
}

tidy_with_vcov <- function(fit, vcov_mat, df) {
  estimates <- stats::coef(fit)
  std_error <- sqrt(diag(vcov_mat))
  statistic <- estimates / std_error
  conf_crit <- stats::qt(0.975, df = df)

  data.frame(
    term = names(estimates),
    estimate = unname(estimates),
    std.error = unname(std_error),
    statistic = unname(statistic),
    p.value = 2 * stats::pt(abs(statistic), df = df, lower.tail = FALSE),
    conf.low = unname(estimates - conf_crit * std_error),
    conf.high = unname(estimates + conf_crit * std_error),
    df = df,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

append_model_meta <- function(tidy_table, model_name, sample_label, n_obs, n_clusters = NA_integer_) {
  out <- tidy_table
  out$model <- model_name
  out$sample <- sample_label
  out$n_obs <- n_obs
  out$n_clusters <- n_clusters

  out[, c(
    "model",
    "sample",
    "term",
    "estimate",
    "std.error",
    "statistic",
    "p.value",
    "conf.low",
    "conf.high",
    "df",
    "n_obs",
    "n_clusters"
  )]
}

extract_term_row <- function(tidy_table, term, design, sample_label, n_obs, n_clusters = NA_integer_) {
  row <- tidy_table[tidy_table$term == term, , drop = FALSE]

  if (!nrow(row)) {
    return(data.frame(
      design = design,
      sample = sample_label,
      term = term,
      estimate = NA_real_,
      std.error = NA_real_,
      statistic = NA_real_,
      p.value = NA_real_,
      conf.low = NA_real_,
      conf.high = NA_real_,
      df = NA_real_,
      n_obs = n_obs,
      n_clusters = n_clusters,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    ))
  }

  row$design <- design
  row$sample <- sample_label
  row$n_obs <- n_obs
  row$n_clusters <- n_clusters

  row[, c(
    "design",
    "sample",
    "term",
    "estimate",
    "std.error",
    "statistic",
    "p.value",
    "conf.low",
    "conf.high",
    "df",
    "n_obs",
    "n_clusters"
  )]
}

fit_lm_plain <- function(formula, data, weights_var = NULL) {
  needed <- unique(c(all.vars(formula), weights_var))
  dat <- data |>
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(needed)) |>
    stats::na.omit()

  if (is.null(weights_var)) {
    fit <- stats::lm(formula, data = dat)
  } else {
    dat$.fit_weights <- dat[[weights_var]]
    fit <- stats::lm(formula, data = dat, weights = .fit_weights)
  }

  tidy <- broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)
  tidy$df <- fit$df.residual

  list(
    fit = fit,
    data = dat,
    tidy = tidy,
    n_obs = nrow(dat)
  )
}

fit_lm_cluster <- function(formula, data, cluster_var) {
  needed <- unique(c(all.vars(formula), cluster_var))
  dat <- data |>
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(needed)) |>
    stats::na.omit()

  fit <- stats::lm(formula, data = dat)
  cluster <- dat[[cluster_var]]
  n_clusters <- dplyr::n_distinct(cluster)
  vcov_mat <- sandwich::vcovCL(fit, cluster = cluster, type = "HC1")
  tidy <- tidy_with_vcov(fit, vcov_mat, df = n_clusters - 1)

  list(
    fit = fit,
    data = dat,
    vcov = vcov_mat,
    tidy = tidy,
    n_obs = nrow(dat),
    n_clusters = n_clusters
  )
}

fit_iv_2sls_cluster <- function(data, outcome, endog, instrument, controls = character(), cluster_var) {
  needed <- unique(c(outcome, endog, instrument, controls, cluster_var))
  dat <- data |>
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(needed)) |>
    stats::na.omit()

  x_formula <- stats::reformulate(c(endog, controls))
  z_formula <- stats::reformulate(c(instrument, controls))

  X <- stats::model.matrix(x_formula, data = dat)
  Z <- stats::model.matrix(z_formula, data = dat)
  y <- matrix(dat[[outcome]], ncol = 1)

  W <- solve(crossprod(Z))
  bread <- t(X) %*% Z %*% W %*% t(Z) %*% X
  beta <- solve(bread, t(X) %*% Z %*% W %*% t(Z) %*% y)
  resid <- y - X %*% beta

  cluster <- dat[[cluster_var]]
  cluster_levels <- unique(cluster)
  S <- matrix(0, ncol(Z), ncol(Z))

  for (cluster_id in cluster_levels) {
    idx <- which(cluster == cluster_id)
    zg <- Z[idx, , drop = FALSE]
    ug <- matrix(resid[idx, , drop = FALSE], ncol = 1)
    mg <- t(zg) %*% ug
    S <- S + mg %*% t(mg)
  }

  vcov_mat <- solve(bread) %*% (t(X) %*% Z %*% W %*% S %*% W %*% t(Z) %*% X) %*% solve(bread)

  n_obs <- nrow(dat)
  n_coef <- ncol(X)
  n_clusters <- length(cluster_levels)
  vcov_mat <- vcov_mat * (n_clusters / (n_clusters - 1)) * ((n_obs - 1) / (n_obs - n_coef))

  estimates <- as.numeric(beta)
  std_error <- sqrt(diag(vcov_mat))
  statistic <- estimates / std_error
  df <- n_clusters - 1
  conf_crit <- stats::qt(0.975, df = df)

  tidy <- data.frame(
    term = colnames(X),
    estimate = estimates,
    std.error = std_error,
    statistic = statistic,
    p.value = 2 * stats::pt(abs(statistic), df = df, lower.tail = FALSE),
    conf.low = estimates - conf_crit * std_error,
    conf.high = estimates + conf_crit * std_error,
    df = df,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )

  first_stage <- fit_lm_cluster(
    stats::reformulate(c(instrument, controls), response = endog),
    data = dat,
    cluster_var = cluster_var
  )

  list(
    data = dat,
    tidy = tidy,
    n_obs = n_obs,
    n_clusters = n_clusters,
    first_stage = first_stage
  )
}

ttest_binary <- function(data, outcome, group_var, sample_label) {
  dat <- data |>
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(c(outcome, group_var))) |>
    stats::na.omit()

  dat <- dat[dat[[group_var]] %in% c(0, 1), , drop = FALSE]
  group_factor <- factor(dat[[group_var]], levels = c(0, 1))
  test <- stats::t.test(dat[[outcome]] ~ group_factor, var.equal = TRUE)
  means <- tapply(dat[[outcome]], dat[[group_var]], mean)
  ns <- table(dat[[group_var]])

  data.frame(
    sample = sample_label,
    outcome = outcome,
    group_var = group_var,
    group0_n = get_named_value(ns, "0"),
    group1_n = get_named_value(ns, "1"),
    group0_mean = get_named_value(means, "0"),
    group1_mean = get_named_value(means, "1"),
    diff_group0_minus_group1 = get_named_value(means, "0") - get_named_value(means, "1"),
    statistic = unname(test$statistic),
    p.value = unname(test$p.value),
    conf.low = unname(test$conf.int[1]),
    conf.high = unname(test$conf.int[2]),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

ttest_many <- function(data, outcomes, group_var, sample_label) {
  dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(outcomes, function(outcome) {
    ttest_binary(data, outcome, group_var, sample_label)
  }))
}

coalesce_pair <- function(left, right) {
  ifelse(is.na(left), right, left)
}

build_matching_wide_data <- function(data) {
  wide <- reshape(
    as.data.frame(data),
    direction = "wide",
    idvar = "household_identifier",
    timevar = "round",
    sep = ""
  )

  keep_vars <- c(
    "locality_identifier",
    "treatment_locality",
    "promotion_locality",
    "eligible",
    "enrolled",
    "enrolled_rp",
    "poverty_index",
    "age_hh",
    "age_sp",
    "educ_hh",
    "educ_sp",
    "female_hh",
    "indigenous",
    "hhsize",
    "dirtfloor",
    "bathroom",
    "land",
    "hospital_distance",
    "hospital"
  )

  for (var_name in keep_vars) {
    wide[[var_name]] <- coalesce_pair(wide[[paste0(var_name, "0")]], wide[[paste0(var_name, "1")]])
  }

  wide[order(wide$household_identifier), ]
}

build_ps_density_plot <- function(data, title) {
  plot_dat <- data.frame(
    pscore = data$pscore,
    group = ifelse(data$enrolled == 1, "Enrolled", "Not enrolled"),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )

  ggplot2::ggplot(plot_dat, ggplot2::aes(x = pscore, color = group, fill = group)) +
    ggplot2::geom_density(alpha = 0.18, linewidth = 0.8) +
    ggplot2::scale_color_manual(values = c("Enrolled" = "#1b4d3e", "Not enrolled" = "#b24a2e")) +
    ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("Enrolled" = "#1b4d3e", "Not enrolled" = "#b24a2e")) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = title,
      x = "Estimated propensity score",
      y = "Density",
      color = NULL,
      fill = NULL
    ) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "top")
}

fit_match_branch <- function(data, covariates, label, seed = 100) {
  needed <- unique(c("enrolled", "health_expenditures0", "health_expenditures1", covariates))
  dat <- data |>
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(needed)) |>
    stats::na.omit()

  ps_formula <- stats::reformulate(covariates, response = "enrolled")
  ps_fit <- stats::glm(ps_formula, data = dat, family = stats::binomial(link = "probit"))
  dat$pscore <- stats::predict(ps_fit, type = "response")

  set.seed(seed)
  match_obj <- MatchIt::matchit(
    ps_formula,
    data = dat,
    method = "nearest",
    distance = "glm",
    link = "probit",
    ratio = 1,
    replace = FALSE,
    m.order = "random",
    estimand = "ATT",
    normalize = FALSE
  )

  matched_dat <- MatchIt::match.data(match_obj)

  support <- data.frame(
    enrolled = dat$enrolled,
    support = as.integer(match_obj$weights > 0),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  ) |>
    dplyr::count(enrolled, support) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      group = ifelse(enrolled == 1, "Enrolled", "Not enrolled"),
      support_label = ifelse(support == 1, "Matched support", "Outside matched support")
    ) |>
    dplyr::select(group, support_label, n)

  weighted_fit <- fit_lm_plain(health_expenditures1 ~ enrolled, matched_dat, weights_var = "weights")

  boot_stat <- function(sample_data, indices) {
    boot_dat <- sample_data[indices, , drop = FALSE]
    boot_dat <- stats::na.omit(boot_dat)

    if (!nrow(boot_dat) || length(unique(boot_dat$enrolled)) < 2) {
      return(NA_real_)
    }

    match_boot <- tryCatch(
      MatchIt::matchit(
        ps_formula,
        data = boot_dat,
        method = "nearest",
        distance = "glm",
        link = "probit",
        ratio = 1,
        replace = FALSE,
        m.order = "random",
        estimand = "ATT",
        normalize = FALSE
      ),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )

    if (is.null(match_boot)) {
      return(NA_real_)
    }

    matched_boot <- tryCatch(MatchIt::match.data(match_boot), error = function(e) NULL)

    if (is.null(matched_boot) || !nrow(matched_boot) || length(unique(matched_boot$enrolled)) < 2) {
      return(NA_real_)
    }

    fit_boot <- stats::lm(health_expenditures1 ~ enrolled, data = matched_boot, weights = weights)
    unname(stats::coef(fit_boot)[["enrolled"]])
  }

  set.seed(seed)
  boot_fit <- boot::boot(dat, statistic = boot_stat, R = 50)
  boot_vals <- as.numeric(boot_fit$t)

  bootstrap_summary <- data.frame(
    design = label,
    bootstrap_reps = 50,
    valid_reps = sum(!is.na(boot_vals)),
    att_original = unname(stats::coef(weighted_fit$fit)[["enrolled"]]),
    bootstrap_mean = mean(boot_vals, na.rm = TRUE),
    bootstrap_se = stats::sd(boot_vals, na.rm = TRUE),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )

  list(
    data = dat,
    ps_fit = ps_fit,
    match_obj = match_obj,
    matched_data = matched_dat,
    support = support,
    weighted_fit = weighted_fit,
    bootstrap = boot_fit,
    bootstrap_summary = bootstrap_summary,
    ps_plot = build_ps_density_plot(dat, paste0(label, ": propensity-score overlap"))
  )
}

compute_icc <- function(data, outcome, cluster_var) {
  needed <- c(outcome, cluster_var)
  dat <- data |>
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(needed)) |>
    stats::na.omit()

  fit <- nlme::lme(
    stats::reformulate("1", response = outcome),
    random = stats::as.formula(paste("~ 1 |", cluster_var)),
    data = dat,
    na.action = stats::na.omit,
    control = nlme::lmeControl(returnObject = TRUE)
  )

  varcorr <- nlme::VarCorr(fit)
  between <- as.numeric(varcorr[1, "Variance"])
  within <- as.numeric(varcorr[nrow(varcorr), "Variance"])
  between / (between + within)
}

two_sample_n_per_arm <- function(sd_outcome, minimum_detectable_effect, power, alpha = 0.05) {
  z_alpha <- stats::qnorm(1 - alpha / 2)
  z_beta <- stats::qnorm(power)

  ceiling(((z_alpha + z_beta)^2 * (sd_outcome^2 + sd_outcome^2)) / minimum_detectable_effect^2)
}

cluster_adjusted_total_n <- function(total_n_no_cluster, rho, num_clusters) {
  total_n <- total_n_no_cluster

  for (iter in seq_len(100)) {
    avg_cluster_size <- total_n / num_clusters
    updated_total_n <- total_n_no_cluster * (1 + (avg_cluster_size - 1) * rho)

    if (abs(updated_total_n - total_n) < 1e-8) {
      total_n <- updated_total_n
      break
    }

    total_n <- updated_total_n
  }

  ceiling(total_n)
}

build_power_table <- function(mean_outcome, sd_outcome, delta_values, power, direction, outcome_label) {
  target_mean <- switch(
    direction,
    decrease = mean_outcome - delta_values,
    increase = mean_outcome + delta_values
  )

  data.frame(
    outcome = outcome_label,
    power = power,
    minimum_detectable_effect = delta_values,
    target_mean = target_mean,
    n_per_arm = vapply(
      delta_values,
      function(delta) two_sample_n_per_arm(sd_outcome, delta, power),
      numeric(1)
    ),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(total_n = 2 * n_per_arm)
}

augment_clustered_power <- function(power_table, rho, num_clusters) {
  power_table |>
    dplyr::rowwise() |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      rho = rho,
      num_clusters = num_clusters,
      total_n_clustered = cluster_adjusted_total_n(total_n, rho, num_clusters),
      n_per_arm_clustered = ceiling(total_n_clustered / 2),
      avg_cluster_size = ceiling(total_n_clustered / num_clusters),
      design_effect = total_n_clustered / total_n
    ) |>
    dplyr::ungroup()
}

method_4_iv_a <- fit_iv_2sls_cluster(
  data = method_4_dat |> dplyr::filter(round == 1),
  outcome = "health_expenditures",
  endog = "enrolled_rp",
  instrument = "promotion_locality",
  controls = character(),
  cluster_var = "locality_identifier"
)

method_4_iv_b <- fit_iv_2sls_cluster(
  data = method_4_dat |> dplyr::filter(round == 1),
  outcome = "health_expenditures",
  endog = "enrolled_rp",
  instrument = "promotion_locality",
  controls = controls,
  cluster_var = "locality_identifier"
)

method_4_first_stage <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  append_model_meta(
    method_4_iv_a$first_stage$tidy,
    "Method 4 first stage: no controls",
    "All households at follow-up",
    method_4_iv_a$first_stage$n_obs,
    method_4_iv_a$first_stage$n_clusters
  ),
  append_model_meta(
    method_4_iv_b$first_stage$tidy,
    "Method 4 first stage: with controls",
    "All households at follow-up",
    method_4_iv_b$first_stage$n_obs,
    method_4_iv_b$first_stage$n_clusters
  )
)

method_4_iv_regressions <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  append_model_meta(
    method_4_iv_a$tidy,
    "Method 4 second stage: no controls",
    "All households at follow-up",
    method_4_iv_a$n_obs,
    method_4_iv_a$n_clusters
  ),
  append_model_meta(
    method_4_iv_b$tidy,
    "Method 4 second stage: with controls",
    "All households at follow-up",
    method_4_iv_b$n_obs,
    method_4_iv_b$n_clusters
  )
)

# ---- Method 5: regression discontinuity ----
method_5_dat <- evaluation_dat |>
  dplyr::filter(treatment_locality == 1) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    poverty_index_left = ifelse(poverty_index <= 58, poverty_index - 58, 0),
    poverty_index_right = ifelse(poverty_index > 58, poverty_index - 58, 0)
  )

method_5_baseline_dat <- method_5_dat |> dplyr::filter(round == 0)
method_5_followup_dat <- method_5_dat |> dplyr::filter(round == 1)

method_5_baseline_fit <- fit_lm_plain(
  health_expenditures ~ poverty_index,
  data = method_5_baseline_dat
)

method_5_baseline_dat$he_pred0 <- stats::predict(method_5_baseline_fit$fit, newdata = method_5_baseline_dat)

method_5_followup_fit_a <- fit_lm_plain(
  health_expenditures ~ poverty_index_left + poverty_index_right + eligible,
  data = method_5_followup_dat
)

method_5_followup_dat$he_pred1 <- stats::predict(method_5_followup_fit_a$fit, newdata = method_5_followup_dat)

method_5_followup_fit_b <- fit_lm_plain(
  stats::reformulate(
    c("eligible", "poverty_index_left", "poverty_index_right", controls),
    response = "health_expenditures"
  ),
  data = method_5_followup_dat
)

method_5_regressions <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  append_model_meta(
    method_5_baseline_fit$tidy,
    "Method 5: baseline health expenditures on poverty index",
    "Treatment localities at baseline",
    method_5_baseline_fit$n_obs
  ),
  append_model_meta(
    method_5_followup_fit_a$tidy,
    "Method 5: RDD without controls",
    "Treatment localities at follow-up",
    method_5_followup_fit_a$n_obs
  ),
  append_model_meta(
    method_5_followup_fit_b$tidy,
    "Method 5: RDD with controls",
    "Treatment localities at follow-up",
    method_5_followup_fit_b$n_obs
  )
)

rdd_density_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(method_5_baseline_dat, ggplot2::aes(x = poverty_index)) +
  ggplot2::geom_density(fill = "#d9e6f2", color = "#24415d", linewidth = 0.8) +
  ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = 58, linetype = "dashed", color = "#b24a2e") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Method 5: baseline poverty-index density",
    x = "Baseline poverty index",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

rdd_participation_dat <- method_5_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(poverty_index) |>
  dplyr::summarise(enrollment_rate = mean(enrolled, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

rdd_participation_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  rdd_participation_dat,
  ggplot2::aes(x = poverty_index, y = enrollment_rate)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(color = "#1b4d3e", alpha = 0.55, size = 1.4) +
  ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = 58, linetype = "dashed", color = "#b24a2e") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Method 5: participation by poverty index",
    x = "Baseline poverty index",
    y = "Participation rate in HISP"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

rdd_followup_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(
  method_5_followup_dat |>
    dplyr::filter(health_expenditures < 60),
  ggplot2::aes(x = poverty_index, y = health_expenditures)
) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.12, size = 1, color = "#24415d") +
  ggplot2::geom_line(
    data = method_5_followup_dat |>
      dplyr::arrange(poverty_index),
    ggplot2::aes(y = he_pred1),
    color = "#b24a2e",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = 58, linetype = "dashed", color = "#3b3b3b") +
  ggplot2::labs(
    title = "Method 5: follow-up health expenditures around the cutoff",
    x = "Baseline poverty index",
    y = "Health expenditures"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

# ---- Method 6: difference-in-differences ----
method_6_dat <- evaluation_dat |>
  dplyr::filter(treatment_locality == 1) |>
  dplyr::mutate(enrolled_round = enrolled * round)

method_6_ttests <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  ttest_binary(
    method_6_dat |> dplyr::filter(enrolled == 0),
    outcome = "health_expenditures",
    group_var = "round",
    sample_label = "Method 6: nonenrolled households"
  ),
  ttest_binary(
    method_6_dat |> dplyr::filter(enrolled == 1),
    outcome = "health_expenditures",
    group_var = "round",
    sample_label = "Method 6: enrolled households"
  )
)

method_6_reg_a <- fit_lm_cluster(
  health_expenditures ~ enrolled_round + round + enrolled,
  data = method_6_dat,
  cluster_var = "locality_identifier"
)

method_6_reg_b <- fit_lm_cluster(
  stats::reformulate(c("enrolled_round", "round", "enrolled", controls), response = "health_expenditures"),
  data = method_6_dat,
  cluster_var = "locality_identifier"
)

method_6_regressions <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  append_model_meta(
    method_6_reg_a$tidy,
    "Method 6: difference-in-differences without controls",
    "Treatment localities, both rounds",
    method_6_reg_a$n_obs,
    method_6_reg_a$n_clusters
  ),
  append_model_meta(
    method_6_reg_b$tidy,
    "Method 6: difference-in-differences with controls",
    "Treatment localities, both rounds",
    method_6_reg_b$n_obs,
    method_6_reg_b$n_clusters
  )
)

# ---- Method 7: propensity-score matching ----
matching_wide_dat <- build_matching_wide_data(evaluation_dat)

matching_wide_summary <- data.frame(
  wide_rows = nrow(matching_wide_dat),
  enrolled_households = sum(matching_wide_dat$enrolled == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
  not_enrolled_households = sum(matching_wide_dat$enrolled == 0, na.rm = TRUE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

matching_restricted <- fit_match_branch(
  matching_wide_dat,
  covariates = matching_controls_restricted,
  label = "Method 7 restricted matching"
)

matching_restricted_support <- matching_restricted$support
matching_restricted_regression <- append_model_meta(
  matching_restricted$weighted_fit$tidy,
  "Method 7 restricted matching: weighted regression",
  "Wide household file, covariates = age_hh + educ_hh",
  matching_restricted$weighted_fit$n_obs
)
matching_restricted_bootstrap <- matching_restricted$bootstrap_summary
matching_restricted_ps_plot <- matching_restricted$ps_plot

matching_full <- fit_match_branch(
  matching_wide_dat,
  covariates = matching_controls_full,
  label = "Method 7 full matching"
)

matching_full_support <- matching_full$support
matching_full_regression <- append_model_meta(
  matching_full$weighted_fit$tidy,
  "Method 7 full matching: weighted regression",
  "Wide household file, covariates = full control set",
  matching_full$weighted_fit$n_obs
)
matching_full_bootstrap <- matching_full$bootstrap_summary
matching_full_ps_plot <- matching_full$ps_plot

matched_did_pairs <- matching_full$matched_data |>
  dplyr::arrange(subclass, dplyr::desc(enrolled)) |>
  dplyr::group_by(subclass) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    treated_baseline = health_expenditures0[enrolled == 1][1],
    treated_followup = health_expenditures1[enrolled == 1][1],
    control_baseline = health_expenditures0[enrolled == 0][1],
    control_followup = health_expenditures1[enrolled == 0][1],
    matched_dd = (treated_followup - treated_baseline) - (control_followup - control_baseline),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

matched_did_manual_summary <- matched_did_pairs |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    matched_pairs = dplyr::n(),
    mean_matched_dd = mean(matched_dd, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_matched_dd = stats::sd(matched_dd, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_matched_dd = min(matched_dd, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_matched_dd = stats::median(matched_dd, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_matched_dd = max(matched_dd, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

matched_did_reg_dat <- matching_full$matched_data |>
  dplyr::mutate(diff = health_expenditures1 - health_expenditures0)

matched_did_reg_fit <- fit_lm_plain(
  diff ~ enrolled,
  data = matched_did_reg_dat,
  weights_var = "weights"
)

matched_did_regression <- append_model_meta(
  matched_did_reg_fit$tidy,
  "Method 7 matched difference-in-differences",
  "Matched full-set sample",
  matched_did_reg_fit$n_obs
)

# ---- Power calculations ----
power_dat <- evaluation_dat |>
  dplyr::filter(eligible == 1)

power_baseline_dat <- power_dat |>
  dplyr::filter(round == 0)

power_followup_treat_dat <- power_dat |>
  dplyr::filter(round == 1, treatment_locality == 1)

health_rho_baseline <- compute_icc(power_baseline_dat, "health_expenditures", "locality_identifier")
hospital_rho_baseline <- compute_icc(power_baseline_dat, "hospital", "locality_identifier")
health_rho_followup <- compute_icc(power_followup_treat_dat, "health_expenditures", "locality_identifier")
hospital_rho_followup <- compute_icc(power_followup_treat_dat, "hospital", "locality_identifier")

power_parameter_summary <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  data.frame(
    outcome = "health_expenditures",
    sample = "Baseline eligible households",
    mean = mean(power_baseline_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = stats::sd(power_baseline_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
    rho = health_rho_baseline,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  ),
  data.frame(
    outcome = "hospital",
    sample = "Baseline eligible households",
    mean = mean(power_baseline_dat$hospital, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = stats::sd(power_baseline_dat$hospital, na.rm = TRUE),
    rho = hospital_rho_baseline,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  ),
  data.frame(
    outcome = "health_expenditures",
    sample = "Follow-up treated eligible households",
    mean = mean(power_followup_treat_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = stats::sd(power_followup_treat_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
    rho = health_rho_followup,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  ),
  data.frame(
    outcome = "hospital",
    sample = "Follow-up treated eligible households",
    mean = mean(power_followup_treat_dat$hospital, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = stats::sd(power_followup_treat_dat$hospital, na.rm = TRUE),
    rho = hospital_rho_followup,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
)

power_health_no_cluster_90 <- build_power_table(
  mean_outcome = mean(power_followup_treat_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
  sd_outcome = stats::sd(power_followup_treat_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
  delta_values = c(1, 2, 3),
  power = 0.9,
  direction = "decrease",
  outcome_label = "Health expenditures"
)

power_health_no_cluster_80 <- build_power_table(
  mean_outcome = mean(power_followup_treat_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
  sd_outcome = stats::sd(power_followup_treat_dat$health_expenditures, na.rm = TRUE),
  delta_values = c(1, 2, 3),
  power = 0.8,
  direction = "decrease",
  outcome_label = "Health expenditures"
)

power_hospital_no_cluster_80 <- build_power_table(
  mean_outcome = mean(power_followup_treat_dat$hospital, na.rm = TRUE),
  sd_outcome = stats::sd(power_followup_treat_dat$hospital, na.rm = TRUE),
  delta_values = c(0.01, 0.02, 0.03),
  power = 0.8,
  direction = "increase",
  outcome_label = "Hospitalization rate"
)

power_health_clustered_100 <- augment_clustered_power(
  power_health_no_cluster_80,
  rho = health_rho_followup,
  num_clusters = 100
)

power_health_clustered_varying_clusters <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(
  c(30, 58, 81, 90, 120),
  function(num_clusters) {
    augment_clustered_power(
      power_health_no_cluster_80 |>
        dplyr::filter(minimum_detectable_effect == 2),
      rho = health_rho_followup,
      num_clusters = num_clusters
    )
  }
))

# ---- Consolidated effect summary ----
effect_pathway_summary <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  extract_term_row(
    method_1_reg_a$tidy,
    "round",
    "Method 1 before-after",
    "Treatment localities, enrolled households",
    method_1_reg_a$n_obs,
    method_1_reg_a$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_1_reg_b$tidy,
    "round",
    "Method 1 before-after + controls",
    "Treatment localities, enrolled households",
    method_1_reg_b$n_obs,
    method_1_reg_b$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_2_reg_a$tidy,
    "enrolled",
    "Method 2 enrolled vs not enrolled",
    "Treatment localities at follow-up",
    method_2_reg_a$n_obs,
    method_2_reg_a$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_2_reg_b$tidy,
    "enrolled",
    "Method 2 enrolled vs not enrolled + controls",
    "Treatment localities at follow-up",
    method_2_reg_b$n_obs,
    method_2_reg_b$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_3_reg_a$tidy,
    "treatment_locality",
    "Method 3 randomized assignment",
    "Eligible households at follow-up",
    method_3_reg_a$n_obs,
    method_3_reg_a$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_3_reg_b$tidy,
    "treatment_locality",
    "Method 3 randomized assignment + controls",
    "Eligible households at follow-up",
    method_3_reg_b$n_obs,
    method_3_reg_b$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_4_iv_a$tidy,
    "enrolled_rp",
    "Method 4 IV",
    "All households at follow-up",
    method_4_iv_a$n_obs,
    method_4_iv_a$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_4_iv_b$tidy,
    "enrolled_rp",
    "Method 4 IV + controls",
    "All households at follow-up",
    method_4_iv_b$n_obs,
    method_4_iv_b$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_5_followup_fit_a$tidy,
    "eligible",
    "Method 5 RDD",
    "Treatment localities at follow-up",
    method_5_followup_fit_a$n_obs
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_5_followup_fit_b$tidy,
    "eligible",
    "Method 5 RDD + controls",
    "Treatment localities at follow-up",
    method_5_followup_fit_b$n_obs
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_6_reg_a$tidy,
    "enrolled_round",
    "Method 6 difference-in-differences",
    "Treatment localities, both rounds",
    method_6_reg_a$n_obs,
    method_6_reg_a$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    method_6_reg_b$tidy,
    "enrolled_round",
    "Method 6 difference-in-differences + controls",
    "Treatment localities, both rounds",
    method_6_reg_b$n_obs,
    method_6_reg_b$n_clusters
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    matching_restricted$weighted_fit$tidy,
    "enrolled",
    "Method 7 restricted matching",
    "Wide household file",
    matching_restricted$weighted_fit$n_obs
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    matching_full$weighted_fit$tidy,
    "enrolled",
    "Method 7 full matching",
    "Wide household file",
    matching_full$weighted_fit$n_obs
  ),
  extract_term_row(
    matched_did_reg_fit$tidy,
    "enrolled",
    "Method 7 matched difference-in-differences",
    "Matched full-set sample",
    matched_did_reg_fit$n_obs
  )
)

title: HISP IE in Practice: Evaluated Code
text: This page runs the evaluated HISP workflow script and shows processed outputs for each branch of the translated Stata replication.

title: HISP IE in Practice: Evaluated Code
text:
1 Evaluated Script
# HISP IE in Practice lab: evaluated code with processed outputs.
# Run with:
#   Rscript docs/labs/hisp_ie_practice_lab_evaluated_code.R

options(width = 120)

get_script_path <- function() {
  for (idx in rev(seq_along(sys.frames()))) {
    if (!is.null(sys.frame(idx)$ofile)) {
      return(normalizePath(sys.frame(idx)$ofile))
    }
  }

  cmd_args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = FALSE)
  file_arg <- "--file="
  matches <- grep(file_arg, cmd_args)

  if (length(matches) > 0) {
    candidate <- sub(file_arg, "", cmd_args[matches[1]])

    if (grepl("hisp_ie_practice_lab_evaluated_code[.]R$", candidate)) {
      return(normalizePath(candidate))
    }
  }

  normalizePath("docs/labs/hisp_ie_practice_lab_evaluated_code.R")
}

script_dir <- dirname(get_script_path())
source(file.path(script_dir, "hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R"), local = TRUE)

message("\n[CSV export manifest]")
print(csv_export_manifest)

message("\n[Data overview]")
print(data_overview)

message("\n[Panel completeness]")
print(panel_completeness)

message("\n[Method effect summary]")
print(effect_pathway_summary)

message("\n[Method 4 first-stage terms]")
print(method_4_first_stage[method_4_first_stage$term %in% c("promotion_locality", "age_hh", "educ_hh"), ])

message("\n[Method 5 RDD coefficients]")
print(method_5_regressions[method_5_regressions$term %in% c("poverty_index", "poverty_index_left", "poverty_index_right", "eligible"), ])

message("\n[Restricted matching bootstrap]")
print(matching_restricted_bootstrap)

message("\n[Full matching bootstrap]")
print(matching_full_bootstrap)

message("\n[Matched DiD summary]")
print(matched_did_manual_summary)

message("\n[Power parameter summary]")
print(power_parameter_summary)

message("\n[Clustered power table]")
print(power_health_clustered_varying_clusters)

message("\nDone: evaluated HISP IE in Practice workflow objects created.")

The HISP lab bundle is substantively aligned with the Stata source. The R code follows the same method order, sample restrictions, core variables, and comparison logic as the main book replication script.

title: Quality Assurance Log: HISP IE in Practice Evaluated Code
text:
3 Detailed QA Log



ID
Evaluated code claim and location
Baseline comparison
Result
Action




H1
The workflow exports every .dta file in data/IE in practice files to .csv before reading evaluation.csv (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R, hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
The do-file opens evaluation.dta directly and does not include a CSV conversion step because it runs in Stata (HISP Book replication.do). The CSV export is required by the user request and preserves the Stata input as the source.
Pass
None


H2
The R script defines the same control set used throughout the do-file (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches Stata global controls age_hh age_sp educ_hh educ_sp female_hh indigenous hhsize dirtfloor bathroom land hospital_distance (HISP Book replication.do).
Pass
None


H3
Method 1 reproduces the treatment-locality, enrolled-household before-after branch with the same mean comparison and clustered regressions (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches the do-file’s sample restriction and models for Method 1 (HISP Book replication.do).
Pass
None


H4
Method 2 reproduces the follow-up enrolled-versus-not-enrolled comparison in treatment localities with clustered regressions and the same controls (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches the do-file’s Method 2 sample restriction and models (HISP Book replication.do).
Pass
None


H5
Method 3 keeps eligible == 1, tests baseline balance, compares baseline and follow-up outcome means, and estimates clustered follow-up regressions (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches the randomized-assignment branch in the do-file (HISP Book replication.do).
Pass
None


H6
Method 4 estimates randomized-promotion IV models using promotion_locality as the instrument for enrolled_rp, and preserves first-stage output (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R, hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches the do-file’s IV setup and the first reporting logic (HISP Book replication.do).
Pass
None


H7
Method 5 recreates the cutoff-normalized left and right running variables, the linear RDD specifications, and the density/participation/follow-up diagnostic plots (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R, hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Aligns with the do-file’s RDD logic and graph sequence (HISP Book replication.do). The exact rdplot rendering is not reproduced because the local R workflow uses ggplot2 diagnostics rather than rdrobust.
Partial alignment
Keep the current plots; only add rdrobust if exact figure replication becomes necessary.


H8
Method 6 reproduces the difference-in-differences branch by building enrolled_round and estimating the same clustered specifications with and without controls (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches the do-file’s DiD section (HISP Book replication.do).
Pass
None


H9
Method 7 reshapes the panel wide, fits a probit propensity score, uses random-order nearest-neighbor matching for the restricted and full covariate sets, and reports support, bootstrap ATT, and weighted regression estimates (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R, hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches the do-file’s matching structure and sequencing (HISP Book replication.do). MatchIt substitutes for psmatch2, but the branch remains probit-based, one-to-one, and random-order nearest neighbor.
Pass
None


H10
The matched difference-in-differences section uses matched subclasses to build pairwise DDs and then fits the weighted diff ~ enrolled regression on the matched sample (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Matches the do-file’s manual matched-DD calculation and regression follow-up (HISP Book replication.do).
Pass
None


H11
The power section computes baseline and follow-up means, standard deviations, ICC estimates, no-cluster sample sizes for the same MDE grids, and clustered sample-size adjustments for the same cluster counts (hisp_ie_practice_lab_full_code.R)
Aligns with the do-file’s Chapter 15 sequence (HISP Book replication.do). The R version uses normal-approximation formulas and an iterative design-effect adjustment instead of Stata’s sampsi and sampclus commands.

Keep the current implementation unless exact sampsi/sampclus parity is required.

title: Quality Assurance Log: HISP IE in Practice Evaluated Code
text:
4 QA Decision
The HISP IE in Practice bundle is ready for use.
It follows the book replication do-file closely enough to support teaching, inspection, and reruns in R. The main deviations are implementation-level substitutions where the Stata-specific commands do not have an identical local R equivalent in the current environment.

title: Quality Assurance Log: HISP IE in Practice Evaluated Code
text:
5 Residual Risks

Matching estimates use MatchIt as the nearest-neighbor engine rather than psmatch2, so very small tie-breaking differences remain possible.
The RDD visuals are analytically aligned but not pixel-identical to the Stata figures that depend on rdplot.
The power-calculation tables are logically aligned with the Stata workflow, but exact numeric parity with sampsi and sampclus is not guaranteed.

title: Synthesis Report: Main Matching Report Versus The Mixtape, The Effect, and Gertler Chapter 8
text:
This synthesis compares the repo’s main report, Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference, with the three companion source-note pages:

The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
The Effect: Matching
Gertler et al., Chapter 8: Matching

The goal is not to rank the sources, but to show where the main report is already well aligned, where it deliberately departs from the books, and where the books suggest useful extensions.

title: Synthesis Report: Main Matching Report Versus The Mixtape, The Effect, and Gertler Chapter 8
text:
This synthesis compares the repo’s main report, Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference, with the three companion source-note pages:

The Mixtape: Matching and Subclassification
The Effect: Matching
Gertler et al., Chapter 8: Matching

The goal is not to rank the sources, but to show where the main report is already well aligned, where it deliberately departs from the books, and where the books suggest useful extensions.

title: Synthesis Report: Main Matching Report Versus The Mixtape, The Effect, and Gertler Chapter 8
text:
2 Executive Synthesis

The main report is closest to The Effect in overall intellectual style: both treat matching as a design problem, stress estimands and weighting logic, and insist that balance must be read alongside overlap and the effective target population.
The main report is closest to The Mixtape in its emphasis on subclassification logic, the curse of dimensionality, and the balance-versus-retention tradeoff, even though the report does not foreground the NSW benchmark case inside the main article.
The main report is closest to Gertler chapter 8 in audience and evaluator workflow: both are written with practical policy evaluation in mind and both insist that matching should be judged against stronger quasi-experimental alternatives rather than treated as a default.
The main report’s biggest deliberate departure from all three sources is its decision to center exact matching, CEM, and entropy balancing while largely bracketing propensity-score matching. That makes the report more auditable and design-transparent, but it also means it leaves out one family of methods that all three source texts discuss directly.
Relative to the books, the main report is stronger on auditability and transparent diagnostics, but weaker on two canonical teaching bridges: subclassification as a standalone entry point and propensity-score methods as the standard high-dimensional workaround.
After implementation of the first recommendation round, that last gap is narrower than it was when this synthesis was first drafted: the main report now includes a subclassification bridge, explicit signposting to the NSW/CPS benchmark, clearer omission logic for propensity scores, an evaluator escalation rule, and a dedicated propensity-score weighting companion note.

title: Synthesis Report: Main Matching Report Versus The Mixtape, The Effect, and Gertler Chapter 8
text:
